1	# Handoff — Laboratory engine implementation
2	
3	The remaining lab work: replacing stub engines with real
4	implementations, codifying the smoke test, retiring the
5	superseded infra spec, and porting the first real-world trial.
6	The apparatus skeleton is complete and verified end-to-end;
7	this handoff is purely incremental implementation.
8	
9	## Where we are
10	
11	Five commits have built and verified the apparatus:
12	
13	  b306ab98  apparatus skeleton
14	  d6387a24  rig template + skeleton engines
15	  776db61f  staged phase orchestrators
16	  65cc30ab  manifest CLI (`nsg lab-trial-post`)
17	  3cc167c1  smoke-test confirmation (live trial in vibers)
18	
19	A trial posted via `nsg lab-trial-post <manifest>` validates,
20	crawls the rig, and resolves cleanly:
21	
22	    setup-phase → scenario-phase → probes-phase →
23	    archive-phase → teardown-phase
24	
25	Each phase orchestrator grafts its work engines from
26	`writ.ext.laboratory.config`. Every work engine is currently a
27	stub (`packages/laboratory/src/engines/stubs.ts`) that logs and
28	yields placeholder data.
29	
30	The smoke test that was run live: trial
31	`w-momen904-e8cd1359f754` in vibers, 13 engines completed in
32	22ms. That confirms the wiring; the engines themselves now need
33	real implementations.
34	
35	## Suggested order
36	
37	The natural sequence is fixtures → scenario → archive design
38	→ probes → archive → teardown. Most engines can be implemented
39	without further design. Probes and the archive engine are
40	blocked on the archive-target design conversation
41	(`.scratch/handoff-laboratory-archive-design.md` — companion
42	handoff; resolve `c-momaa5o9` before steps 4–5 below).
43	
44	The rest is mechanical: each engine has a stub at
45	`stubs.ts` showing the contract; replace it with a real
46	implementation (its own file under `engines/`); add tests.
47	
48	### 1. `lab.codex-setup` + `lab.codex-teardown` (`c-moma9y1k`)
49	
50	**Setup:** clone upstream codex at the configured base SHA
51	into a temp dir; push to a fresh GH private repo named
52	`trial-<slug>-<trialId-prefix>` in the same org as the
53	upstream codex (default: `shardworks`); register the new repo
54	as a guild codex via `nsg codex add` (or call `ScriptoriumApi`
55	directly — the engine is in-process and can use the apparatus
56	API).
57	
58	**Teardown:** `gh repo delete --yes`. Safety check: refuse
59	to delete if `lab.archive` hasn't run for this rig (read the
60	rig's engine list; check archive engine status). The check
61	happens in the engine code; the design click `c-mom19282`
62	established this discipline.
63	
64	**Givens (manifest shape):**
65	```yaml
66	fixtures:
67	  - id: codex
68	    engineId: lab.codex-setup
69	    givens:
70	      upstreamRepo: shardworks/nexus     # owner/name
71	      baseSha: <40-char>
72	      # codex name in lab guild defaults to fixture id; override if needed:
73	      # codexName: experiment-codex-001
74	```
75	
76	**Yields:**
77	```ts
78	{
79	  codexName: string,      // the lab-guild-side codex name
80	  remoteUrl: string,      // GH URL of the new repo
81	  baseSha: string,        // echoed for downstream consumption
82	  headSha: string,        // = baseSha at setup time; updated by scenario
83	  ownerRepo: string,      // 'shardworks/trial-<slug>-<id-prefix>'
84	}
85	```
86	
87	**Yellow flag from recon:** the `gh` token has `repo` scope
88	and active membership in `shardworks` but `admin: false` on
89	existing repos. Member-policy for repo create + delete
90	unverified. First real run is the verification — be ready to
91	back off to a different namespace (e.g. user-account) if the
92	org rejects the create. The recon doc:
93	`.scratch/laboratory-framework-recon.md`.
94	
95	**Trial id prefix derivation:** drop the `w-` prefix and
96	take the first 8 chars of the timestamp portion. So
97	`w-momen904-e8cd1359f754` → `momen904`. Repo name:
98	`trial-<slug>-momen904`.
99	
100	### 2. `lab.guild-setup` + `lab.guild-teardown` (`c-momaa03d`)
101	
102	**Setup (the gnarliest engine):**
103	
104	1. Refuse if the target guild dir already exists.
105	2. `nsg init <guild-dir>` (verify exact CLI surface against
106	   `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/commands/init.ts`
107	   — does it want `--name`, `--model` flags?).
108	3. For each plugin pin in `givens.plugins`:
109	   `nsg plugin install <name>@<version>`. The version field
110	   accepts npm semver, dist-tags, git URLs with `#<sha>`, and
111	   local paths — pass through to npm unchanged.
112	4. `nsg codex add <name> <remoteUrl>` — read codex info
113	   from upstream context.upstream['fixture-codex-setup'] yields
114	   (or whatever the codex fixture id is). The engine should
115	   look up its dependsOn target's yields, not require redundant
116	   manifest config.
117	5. Deep-merge `givens.config` into `<guild-dir>/guild.json`.
118	6. Copy each entry of `givens.files` from sourcePath
119	   (relative to manifest dir? sanctum root? — decide and
120	   document) to guildPath (relative to guild root).
121	
122	**Teardown:** `rm -rf <guild-dir>`. Same archive safety check
123	as codex-teardown.
124	
125	**Givens (manifest shape):**
126	```yaml
127	fixtures:
128	  - id: test-guild
129	    engineId: lab.guild-setup
130	    dependsOn: [codex]       # so codex setup yields are available
131	    givens:
132	      # guild path defaults to /workspace/experiments/<trial-slug>-<id-prefix>/
133	      # plugins: pin every plugin the test guild needs
134	      plugins:
135	        - name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus'
136	          version: <SHA-or-version>
137	        - name: '@shardworks/animator-apparatus'
138	          version: <SHA-or-version>
139	      # config: partial guild.json merge
140	      config:
141	        astrolabe:
142	          patronRole: patron
143	      # files: copy into the guild
144	      files:
145	        - sourcePath: prompts/handoff-strong.md
146	          guildPath: animator/instructions/implement.md
147	```
148	
149	**Yields:**
150	```ts
151	{
152	  guildPath: string,       // absolute path of the test guild
153	  pluginsResolved: Array<{ name: string; version: string }>,
154	  filesCopied: Array<{ sourcePath: string; guildPath: string }>,
155	}
156	```
157	
158	**Source-path resolution:** decide whether `sourcePath` is
159	relative to (a) the manifest file's dir, (b) the sanctum root,
160	or (c) absolute paths only. The earlier infra spec had
161	manifest-relative; pick that unless there's a reason to differ.
162	
163	### 3. `lab.commission-post-xguild` + `lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild` (`c-momaa1vt`)
164	
165	Cross-guild action via shell-out. The lab-host guild is
166	running this engine; the test guild is the target. Use
167	`nsg --guild-root <test-guild-path> <command>` to talk to it
168	— same CLI surface, just routed elsewhere.
169	
170	**`lab.commission-post-xguild`:**
171	
172	```ts
173	// givens
174	{
175	  briefPath: string;       // relative to manifest? sanctum? — match guild-setup convention
176	  type?: string;           // writ type, default 'mandate'
177	  // codex / parentId / etc. as needed
178	}
179	
180	// yields
181	{
182	  writId: string;          // commission writ id in the test guild
183	  postedAt: string;
184	}
185	```
186	
187	Implementation: read brief content, exec
188	`nsg --guild-root <yields.fixture-test-guild-setup.guildPath>
189	commission-post --title <title> --body <body> --type <type>`,
190	parse the JSON response, return the writ id.
191	
192	**`lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild`:**
193	
194	```ts
195	// givens
196	{
197	  writId: string;          // typically `${yields.scenario.writId}` (intra-rig); for
198	                           // multi-step scenarios, an explicit ref
199	  pollIntervalMs?: number; // default 5000
200	  timeoutMs?: number;      // default 30 minutes? — pick a sensible cap
201	}
202	
203	// yields
204	{
205	  writId: string;
206	  finalState: 'completed' | 'failed' | 'cancelled';
207	  resolution: string;
208	  resolvedAt: string;
209	}
210	```
211	
212	Implementation: poll
213	`nsg --guild-root <test-guild> writ show <writId>` at the
214	configured interval until state is terminal. Bound by timeout.
215	Fail-loud on timeout (engine fails).
216	
217	**Note on shell-out:** the design parked
218	`c-mom9vm3n` (real cross-guild engine surface) for v2; v1 is
219	shell-out via the CLI. Watch for: auth boundaries (none — same
220	filesystem, same user), perf (process-spawn cost per poll —
221	acceptable for v1; revisit if it bottlenecks), observability
222	(no clean way to stream the test-guild's output back; we just
223	get the JSON response).
224	
225	### 4. *[ARCHIVE DESIGN BLOCKING]* — resolve `c-momaa5o9`
226	
227	Stop and pull the archive design conversation
228	(`.scratch/handoff-laboratory-archive-design.md`).
229	
230	**Why probes and archive are both blocked here:** probes
231	write data; the archive engine packages it. Where data lives
232	determines what probes write. If the archive home is the lab
233	guild's books, probes write rows. If the archive home is the
234	sanctum filesystem, probes write files. If hybrid, both.
235	
236	This blocks both probe implementations and the archive engine
237	itself. Don't try to ship probes against the stub archive
238	contract — the contract is what the design conversation has to
239	fix.
240	
241	### 5. `lab.probe-stacks-dump` + `lab.probe-git-range` (`c-momaa3w7`)
242	
243	Implementations depend on the archive design.
244	
245	**`lab.probe-stacks-dump`:** export the test guild's books
246	to the archive target. The earlier infra spec said
247	JSON-per-table with a >50KB blob carve-out. That shape
248	survives if archive home is the filesystem (option B); under
249	A or C the equivalent is rows in lab-guild books.
250	
251	**`lab.probe-git-range`:** capture commits between the codex
252	base SHA and head SHA. Output: per-commit diffs +
253	commits-manifest.yaml. Generic — the spec said "useful beyond
254	this infrastructure" — so consider promoting later.
255	
256	Both probes read the upstream guild path / codex info from
257	their context.upstream (the fixture yields) — explicit
258	declared upstreams in their grafted entry, not implicit
259	discovery.
260	
261	### 6. `lab.archive` (`c-momaa5o9` impl)
262	
263	Final consolidation step. Design dictates shape. Yields
264	should include archive-target identifier (writ id if A/C,
265	filesystem path if B) so the trial writ's resolution carries
266	forward where to find the archive.
267	
268	After this engine completes, codex-teardown and guild-teardown
269	are safe to run. Encode the safety check by reading the rig's
270	engine list and verifying `archive`'s status.
271	
272	### 7. Smoke test as a package test (`c-momaa75l`)
273	
274	Codify the live smoke test as a recurring test in the
275	laboratory package:
276	
277	  - Builds a synthetic guild in tmp.
278	  - Posts a trivial trial via the CLI tool.
279	  - Asserts the rig completes with all expected engines in
280	    the expected ordering.
281	  - Cleans up.
282	
283	Look at how spider's integration tests build mock guilds for
284	precedent (`/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/spider/src/
285	spider-test-fixture.ts` may be the reusable shape).
286	
287	### 8. Retire the superseded infra spec (`c-momaa8mk`)
288	
289	`experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md` is the
290	spec the apparatus replaces. Delete or archive it (decide:
291	historical-archive vs. delete-since-superseded). Conclude
292	`c-molveg89` (the click that tracked the spec as a P3
293	prerequisite) with a pointer to the apparatus.
294	
295	### 9. Documentation (`c-momaaa3t`)
296	
297	- `packages/laboratory/README.md` — already touched in earlier
298	  commits; flesh out manifest authoring, the rig flow, the
299	  archive layout (per the design conversation).
300	- `docs/architecture/apparatus/laboratory.md` (or wherever
301	  apparatus docs live in `/workspace/nexus/docs/architecture/`)
302	  — describe the apparatus's place in the architecture.
303	- A user-facing guide: "evaluating prompts/plugins via lab
304	  trials." Live in the sanctum, since end-users are the
305	  audience and they consume sanctum-side docs.
306	
307	### 10. Port the first real-world trial (`c-momaab8y`)
308	
309	Pick the next P3 experiment (likely X016 orientation
310	suppression — see the cost-optimization landscape for the
311	candidate list). Author its manifest, run it, verify findings
312	land where expected.
313	
314	This is the capstone — validates the apparatus on real
315	research work and produces the first real findings via the
316	new pipeline.
317	
318	## Implementation discipline
319	
320	- **One commit per engine.** Each commit ships the engine
321	  + its tests + replaces the stub in `stubs.ts` + adds the
322	  real implementation file under `engines/`. Conclude the
323	  matching click in the same commit.
324	- **Match the framework's conventions.** Each engine is an
325	  `EngineDesign` with `id`, `run(givens, context)`, and
326	  optional `retry`. Errors throw; the spider routes to engine
327	  failure.
328	- **Givens carry the contract.** Document each engine's
329	  givens shape in a TS comment block at the top of the file.
330	  Manifest authors read those to know what to put in
331	  `ext.laboratory.config`.
332	- **Live verification per engine.** After implementing each
333	  engine, post a trial that exercises it and check the rig's
334	  completion. The smoke-test pattern (write tmp manifest,
335	  `nsg lab-trial-post`, `nsg rig show`, clean up) works for
336	  one-off verification before the codified test lands.
337	
338	## Pointers to read first
339	
340	- `nsg click extract --id c-moma9llq` — full implementation
341	  click subtree
342	- `packages/laboratory/src/engines/phases.ts` — the
343	  orchestrators that route into each engine
344	- `packages/laboratory/src/engines/stubs.ts` — every engine's
345	  current placeholder + its conceptual summary
346	- `packages/laboratory/src/types.ts` —
347	  `LaboratoryTrialConfig` + decl shapes
348	- `packages/laboratory/src/manifest.ts` — manifest schema +
349	  validator
350	- `.scratch/laboratory-framework-recon.md` — framework
351	  prerequisite findings (still relevant)
352	- `experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md` — the
353	  earlier standalone-bash spec; its directory layout and
354	  step-by-step setup script is detailed reference material
355	  for the fixture engines
356	
357	## Live test environment
358	
359	Vibers is the lab-host guild for development. After each
360	plugin code change, vibers' daemon needs a restart to pick up
361	the new engine registrations. The CLI tool (`nsg
362	lab-trial-post`) reads the latest source per-invocation
363	(fresh node process), but the running spider in the daemon
364	loaded its engines at startup.
365	
366	The `gh` CLI is authenticated against `seatec26` with `repo`
367	scope; org membership in `shardworks` is active. First real
368	codex-setup run is the verification of repo create/delete
369	permissions — surface to Sean if the create or delete is
370	denied.
371	
372	## Companion handoff
373	
374	`.scratch/handoff-laboratory-archive-design.md` — the design
375	conversation that unblocks steps 4–5 above.
376

# Handoff — Laboratory engine implementation

The remaining lab work: replacing stub engines with real
implementations, codifying the smoke test, retiring the
superseded infra spec, and porting the first real-world trial.
The apparatus skeleton is complete and verified end-to-end;
this handoff is purely incremental implementation.

## Where we are

Five commits have built and verified the apparatus:

  b306ab98  apparatus skeleton
  d6387a24  rig template + skeleton engines
  776db61f  staged phase orchestrators
  65cc30ab  manifest CLI (`nsg lab-trial-post`)
  3cc167c1  smoke-test confirmation (live trial in vibers)

A trial posted via `nsg lab-trial-post <manifest>` validates,
crawls the rig, and resolves cleanly:

    setup-phase → scenario-phase → probes-phase →
    archive-phase → teardown-phase

Each phase orchestrator grafts its work engines from
`writ.ext.laboratory.config`. Every work engine is currently a
stub (`packages/laboratory/src/engines/stubs.ts`) that logs and
yields placeholder data.

The smoke test that was run live: trial
`w-momen904-e8cd1359f754` in vibers, 13 engines completed in
22ms. That confirms the wiring; the engines themselves now need
real implementations.

## Suggested order

The natural sequence is fixtures → scenario → archive design
→ probes → archive → teardown. Most engines can be implemented
without further design. Probes and the archive engine are
blocked on the archive-target design conversation
(`.scratch/handoff-laboratory-archive-design.md` — companion
handoff; resolve `c-momaa5o9` before steps 4–5 below).

The rest is mechanical: each engine has a stub at
`stubs.ts` showing the contract; replace it with a real
implementation (its own file under `engines/`); add tests.

### 1. `lab.codex-setup` + `lab.codex-teardown` (`c-moma9y1k`)

**Setup:** clone upstream codex at the configured base SHA
into a temp dir; push to a fresh GH private repo named
`trial-<slug>-<trialId-prefix>` in the same org as the
upstream codex (default: `shardworks`); register the new repo
as a guild codex via `nsg codex add` (or call `ScriptoriumApi`
directly — the engine is in-process and can use the apparatus
API).

**Teardown:** `gh repo delete --yes`. Safety check: refuse
to delete if `lab.archive` hasn't run for this rig (read the
rig's engine list; check archive engine status). The check
happens in the engine code; the design click `c-mom19282`
established this discipline.

**Givens (manifest shape):**
```yaml
fixtures:
  - id: codex
    engineId: lab.codex-setup
    givens:
      upstreamRepo: shardworks/nexus     # owner/name
      baseSha: <40-char>
      # codex name in lab guild defaults to fixture id; override if needed:
      # codexName: experiment-codex-001
```

**Yields:**
```ts
{
  codexName: string,      // the lab-guild-side codex name
  remoteUrl: string,      // GH URL of the new repo
  baseSha: string,        // echoed for downstream consumption
  headSha: string,        // = baseSha at setup time; updated by scenario
  ownerRepo: string,      // 'shardworks/trial-<slug>-<id-prefix>'
}
```

**Yellow flag from recon:** the `gh` token has `repo` scope
and active membership in `shardworks` but `admin: false` on
existing repos. Member-policy for repo create + delete
unverified. First real run is the verification — be ready to
back off to a different namespace (e.g. user-account) if the
org rejects the create. The recon doc:
`.scratch/laboratory-framework-recon.md`.

**Trial id prefix derivation:** drop the `w-` prefix and
take the first 8 chars of the timestamp portion. So
`w-momen904-e8cd1359f754` → `momen904`. Repo name:
`trial-<slug>-momen904`.

### 2. `lab.guild-setup` + `lab.guild-teardown` (`c-momaa03d`)

**Setup (the gnarliest engine):**

1. Refuse if the target guild dir already exists.
2. `nsg init <guild-dir>` (verify exact CLI surface against
   `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/commands/init.ts`
   — does it want `--name`, `--model` flags?).
3. For each plugin pin in `givens.plugins`:
   `nsg plugin install <name>@<version>`. The version field
   accepts npm semver, dist-tags, git URLs with `#<sha>`, and
   local paths — pass through to npm unchanged.
4. `nsg codex add <name> <remoteUrl>` — read codex info
   from upstream context.upstream['fixture-codex-setup'] yields
   (or whatever the codex fixture id is). The engine should
   look up its dependsOn target's yields, not require redundant
   manifest config.
5. Deep-merge `givens.config` into `<guild-dir>/guild.json`.
6. Copy each entry of `givens.files` from sourcePath
   (relative to manifest dir? sanctum root? — decide and
   document) to guildPath (relative to guild root).

**Teardown:** `rm -rf <guild-dir>`. Same archive safety check
as codex-teardown.

**Givens (manifest shape):**
```yaml
fixtures:
  - id: test-guild
    engineId: lab.guild-setup
    dependsOn: [codex]       # so codex setup yields are available
    givens:
      # guild path defaults to /workspace/experiments/<trial-slug>-<id-prefix>/
      # plugins: pin every plugin the test guild needs
      plugins:
        - name: '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus'
          version: <SHA-or-version>
        - name: '@shardworks/animator-apparatus'
          version: <SHA-or-version>
      # config: partial guild.json merge
      config:
        astrolabe:
          patronRole: patron
      # files: copy into the guild
      files:
        - sourcePath: prompts/handoff-strong.md
          guildPath: animator/instructions/implement.md
```

**Yields:**
```ts
{
  guildPath: string,       // absolute path of the test guild
  pluginsResolved: Array<{ name: string; version: string }>,
  filesCopied: Array<{ sourcePath: string; guildPath: string }>,
}
```

**Source-path resolution:** decide whether `sourcePath` is
relative to (a) the manifest file's dir, (b) the sanctum root,
or (c) absolute paths only. The earlier infra spec had
manifest-relative; pick that unless there's a reason to differ.

### 3. `lab.commission-post-xguild` + `lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild` (`c-momaa1vt`)

Cross-guild action via shell-out. The lab-host guild is
running this engine; the test guild is the target. Use
`nsg --guild-root <test-guild-path> <command>` to talk to it
— same CLI surface, just routed elsewhere.

**`lab.commission-post-xguild`:**

```ts
// givens
{
  briefPath: string;       // relative to manifest? sanctum? — match guild-setup convention
  type?: string;           // writ type, default 'mandate'
  // codex / parentId / etc. as needed
}

// yields
{
  writId: string;          // commission writ id in the test guild
  postedAt: string;
}
```

Implementation: read brief content, exec
`nsg --guild-root <yields.fixture-test-guild-setup.guildPath>
commission-post --title <title> --body <body> --type <type>`,
parse the JSON response, return the writ id.

**`lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild`:**

```ts
// givens
{
  writId: string;          // typically `${yields.scenario.writId}` (intra-rig); for
                           // multi-step scenarios, an explicit ref
  pollIntervalMs?: number; // default 5000
  timeoutMs?: number;      // default 30 minutes? — pick a sensible cap
}

// yields
{
  writId: string;
  finalState: 'completed' | 'failed' | 'cancelled';
  resolution: string;
  resolvedAt: string;
}
```

Implementation: poll
`nsg --guild-root <test-guild> writ show <writId>` at the
configured interval until state is terminal. Bound by timeout.
Fail-loud on timeout (engine fails).

**Note on shell-out:** the design parked
`c-mom9vm3n` (real cross-guild engine surface) for v2; v1 is
shell-out via the CLI. Watch for: auth boundaries (none — same
filesystem, same user), perf (process-spawn cost per poll —
acceptable for v1; revisit if it bottlenecks), observability
(no clean way to stream the test-guild's output back; we just
get the JSON response).

### 4. *[ARCHIVE DESIGN BLOCKING]* — resolve `c-momaa5o9`

Stop and pull the archive design conversation
(`.scratch/handoff-laboratory-archive-design.md`).

**Why probes and archive are both blocked here:** probes
write data; the archive engine packages it. Where data lives
determines what probes write. If the archive home is the lab
guild's books, probes write rows. If the archive home is the
sanctum filesystem, probes write files. If hybrid, both.

This blocks both probe implementations and the archive engine
itself. Don't try to ship probes against the stub archive
contract — the contract is what the design conversation has to
fix.

### 5. `lab.probe-stacks-dump` + `lab.probe-git-range` (`c-momaa3w7`)

Implementations depend on the archive design.

**`lab.probe-stacks-dump`:** export the test guild's books
to the archive target. The earlier infra spec said
JSON-per-table with a >50KB blob carve-out. That shape
survives if archive home is the filesystem (option B); under
A or C the equivalent is rows in lab-guild books.

**`lab.probe-git-range`:** capture commits between the codex
base SHA and head SHA. Output: per-commit diffs +
commits-manifest.yaml. Generic — the spec said "useful beyond
this infrastructure" — so consider promoting later.

Both probes read the upstream guild path / codex info from
their context.upstream (the fixture yields) — explicit
declared upstreams in their grafted entry, not implicit
discovery.

### 6. `lab.archive` (`c-momaa5o9` impl)

Final consolidation step. Design dictates shape. Yields
should include archive-target identifier (writ id if A/C,
filesystem path if B) so the trial writ's resolution carries
forward where to find the archive.

After this engine completes, codex-teardown and guild-teardown
are safe to run. Encode the safety check by reading the rig's
engine list and verifying `archive`'s status.

### 7. Smoke test as a package test (`c-momaa75l`)

Codify the live smoke test as a recurring test in the
laboratory package:

  - Builds a synthetic guild in tmp.
  - Posts a trivial trial via the CLI tool.
  - Asserts the rig completes with all expected engines in
    the expected ordering.
  - Cleans up.

Look at how spider's integration tests build mock guilds for
precedent (`/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/spider/src/
spider-test-fixture.ts` may be the reusable shape).

### 8. Retire the superseded infra spec (`c-momaa8mk`)

`experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md` is the
spec the apparatus replaces. Delete or archive it (decide:
historical-archive vs. delete-since-superseded). Conclude
`c-molveg89` (the click that tracked the spec as a P3
prerequisite) with a pointer to the apparatus.

### 9. Documentation (`c-momaaa3t`)

- `packages/laboratory/README.md` — already touched in earlier
  commits; flesh out manifest authoring, the rig flow, the
  archive layout (per the design conversation).
- `docs/architecture/apparatus/laboratory.md` (or wherever
  apparatus docs live in `/workspace/nexus/docs/architecture/`)
  — describe the apparatus's place in the architecture.
- A user-facing guide: "evaluating prompts/plugins via lab
  trials." Live in the sanctum, since end-users are the
  audience and they consume sanctum-side docs.

### 10. Port the first real-world trial (`c-momaab8y`)

Pick the next P3 experiment (likely X016 orientation
suppression — see the cost-optimization landscape for the
candidate list). Author its manifest, run it, verify findings
land where expected.

This is the capstone — validates the apparatus on real
research work and produces the first real findings via the
new pipeline.

## Implementation discipline

- **One commit per engine.** Each commit ships the engine
  + its tests + replaces the stub in `stubs.ts` + adds the
  real implementation file under `engines/`. Conclude the
  matching click in the same commit.
- **Match the framework's conventions.** Each engine is an
  `EngineDesign` with `id`, `run(givens, context)`, and
  optional `retry`. Errors throw; the spider routes to engine
  failure.
- **Givens carry the contract.** Document each engine's
  givens shape in a TS comment block at the top of the file.
  Manifest authors read those to know what to put in
  `ext.laboratory.config`.
- **Live verification per engine.** After implementing each
  engine, post a trial that exercises it and check the rig's
  completion. The smoke-test pattern (write tmp manifest,
  `nsg lab-trial-post`, `nsg rig show`, clean up) works for
  one-off verification before the codified test lands.

## Pointers to read first

- `nsg click extract --id c-moma9llq` — full implementation
  click subtree
- `packages/laboratory/src/engines/phases.ts` — the
  orchestrators that route into each engine
- `packages/laboratory/src/engines/stubs.ts` — every engine's
  current placeholder + its conceptual summary
- `packages/laboratory/src/types.ts` —
  `LaboratoryTrialConfig` + decl shapes
- `packages/laboratory/src/manifest.ts` — manifest schema +
  validator
- `.scratch/laboratory-framework-recon.md` — framework
  prerequisite findings (still relevant)
- `experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md` — the
  earlier standalone-bash spec; its directory layout and
  step-by-step setup script is detailed reference material
  for the fixture engines

## Live test environment

Vibers is the lab-host guild for development. After each
plugin code change, vibers' daemon needs a restart to pick up
the new engine registrations. The CLI tool (`nsg
lab-trial-post`) reads the latest source per-invocation
(fresh node process), but the running spider in the daemon
loaded its engines at startup.

The `gh` CLI is authenticated against `seatec26` with `repo`
scope; org membership in `shardworks` is active. First real
codex-setup run is the verification of repo create/delete
permissions — surface to Sean if the create or delete is
denied.

## Companion handoff

`.scratch/handoff-laboratory-archive-design.md` — the design
conversation that unblocks steps 4–5 above.

1	# Handoff — Laboratory archive design conversation
2	
3	A focused design conversation to resolve the open archive-target
4	question (click `c-momaa5o9`). Output is a decision plus enough
5	specificity that the archive engine and the probe engines can be
6	implemented without further design pings.
7	
8	## Where we are
9	
10	The Laboratory apparatus is built and operable end-to-end with stub
11	engines:
12	
13	- Trial writ type, manifest CLI (`nsg lab-trial-post`), and the
14	  `post-and-collect-default` rig template are wired and live.
15	- Five phase orchestrators (setup → scenario → probes → archive
16	  → teardown) graft their per-phase engines from the trial config.
17	- All work engines are stubs that yield placeholder data; rigs
18	  crawl through cleanly. Smoke-test verified live in vibers.
19	
20	Live trial run, for orientation:
21	
22	    nsg --guild-root /workspace/vibers writ show w-momen904
23	    nsg --guild-root /workspace/vibers rig show rig-momencrs-bfc587fd
24	
25	## The decision in one frame
26	
27	Where do the data the lab CAPTURES live? Three artifact streams:
28	
29	- **Stacks dump** — the test guild's books exported (writs, links,
30	  sessions, plus whatever new books). Currently produced by
31	  `probe-stacks-dump`. Sean's earlier infra spec said
32	  JSON-per-table.
33	- **Codex history** — per-commit diffs + a commits-manifest
34	  between the codex base and head SHAs. Currently produced by
35	  `probe-git-range`. Sean's earlier spec said git format-patch
36	  output.
37	- **Probe metadata + cross-references** — counts, the
38	  scenario writ id, plugin pin info, etc. Lighter; doesn't have
39	  the same scale as the dumps.
40	
41	## The three options
42	
43	### A. Lab-host guild's books
44	
45	Captured data lives natively in the lab guild's stacks. New books
46	register: `lab-trial-archives` (parent record per trial),
47	`lab-trial-stacks-dumps` (rows from the test guild's books, tagged
48	by source-book + source-row-id), `lab-trial-codex-commits` (one
49	row per captured commit, body = the diff).
50	
51	- **Pro:** the lab guild becomes self-describing — a single
52	  query surface for all trial data; supports cross-trial
53	  analysis without filesystem walking.
54	- **Pro:** the books contract handles schema evolution
55	  (`books_<plugin>_<book>_<sha>`), retention, indexing.
56	- **Pro:** survives sanctum reorganization (X-num renumbering,
57	  experiment migration) without rewriting paths.
58	- **Con:** schema design work for capture-shaped data —
59	  particularly the stacks-dump shape, since rows from many books
60	  with different schemas need a common storage shape.
61	- **Con:** large diffs as TEXT in SQLite is fine but limits
62	  scale; would need a blob-extraction policy at some threshold.
63	- **Con:** loses the filesystem-as-archive intuition the
64	  original infra spec built around. Browsing trial results means
65	  going through nsg / Oculus rather than `cd experiments/X...`.
66	
67	### B. Sanctum mirror
68	
69	Captured data lands as files under
70	`experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/trial-<NNN>-<variant>/`.
71	Same shape as the original infrastructure spec (the one this
72	apparatus supersedes): stacks-export/, codex-history/, run.yaml,
73	README.md.
74	
75	- **Pro:** continuity with the existing infra spec — the spec
76	  text already describes this layout in detail; adoption is
77	  basically "make the apparatus produce what the bash scripts
78	  would have."
79	- **Pro:** browsable. `cd experiments/X016/artifacts/trial-001-…/`
80	  and you see everything.
81	- **Pro:** filesystem is the archive. No DB schema migration
82	  concerns; old trials stay readable as long as the directory
83	  tree exists.
84	- **Con:** two homes for what's conceptually one body of data
85	  (writ in the lab guild's books pointing at filesystem paths).
86	- **Con:** filesystem-as-database friction — cross-trial
87	  queries mean walking directories with shell tools or building a
88	  static index.
89	- **Con:** the `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/` tree was built when
90	  experiments were patron-authored. With the lab apparatus
91	  generating runs, that tree's shape may need to evolve.
92	- **Con:** sanctum and lab-host guild may not be the same
93	  filesystem — adds a "sanctum path" config to the lab apparatus
94	  (not necessarily co-located).
95	
96	### C. Hybrid
97	
98	Books in the lab guild hold a small per-trial archive record
99	(metadata + paths/pointers); contents (stacks dump + codex
100	diffs) live as files in a configured archive root. The trial
101	writ links to its archive record via a kit-contributed link
102	kind (`laboratory.archived-as`).
103	
104	- **Pro:** queryable provenance + filesystem browsability. Best
105	  of both.
106	- **Pro:** filesystem can be the sanctum tree (continuity with
107	  the spec) OR a dedicated archive volume — the path is a
108	  guild config, not hard-coded.
109	- **Con:** more moving parts. Archive engine writes both books
110	  and filesystem; teardown safety check has two surfaces to
111	  verify.
112	- **Con:** "where does this trial's data live?" has two
113	  answers. Tools must know to consult both.
114	
115	## What the conversation must produce
116	
117	1. **Pick A, B, or C.**
118	2. **Storage layout** — the concrete shape:
119	   - For (A): exact book schemas (lab-trial-archives,
120	     lab-trial-stacks-dumps, lab-trial-codex-commits — or
121	     whatever set we settle on).
122	   - For (B): exact directory layout (does the existing
123	     `experiments/X<NNN>/artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/` shape
124	     survive verbatim, or does it evolve?).
125	   - For (C): both schemas + the path-config field on the
126	     laboratory apparatus.
127	3. **Trial writ → archive linkage** — how does
128	   `nsg writ show <trial-id>` surface the archive location?
129	   Most likely a `laboratory.archived-as` link kind contributed
130	   to the Clerk; targets are either an archive-record writ id
131	   (A or C) or an archive path (B; opaque string in the link
132	   `kind`).
133	4. **Stacks-dump row shape (if A or C with books)** — the
134	   test guild's books have heterogeneous schemas. How is one
135	   row from `clerk/writs` stored alongside one row from
136	   `animator/sessions`? Most plausible: a `book` + `row` JSON
137	   blob per dump entry, indexed on `(trialId, sourceBook,
138	   sourceRowId)`. Worth confirming.
139	5. **Probe engine output contract** — once we know where
140	   archives go, the probe yields shape is fixed. Probes yield
141	   either:
142	   - paths (`{ files: [{ path, size, sha }] }`) → archive
143	     engine packages them
144	   - book row counts + ids (`{ written: { writs: 12, … } }`) →
145	     archive engine consolidates
146	   - both, depending on probe.
147	
148	## Pointers to read first
149	
150	- `nsg click extract --id c-momaa5o9` — the archive click
151	- `nsg click extract --id c-mom18vmh` — full apparatus design
152	  context
153	- `experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md` — the
154	  earlier standalone-bash spec; its filesystem layout is the
155	  starting point for option B
156	- `packages/laboratory/src/types.ts` — current
157	  `TrialArchiveDecl` shape (provisional)
158	- `packages/laboratory/src/engines/stubs.ts` — what `lab.archive`
159	  currently does (logs + yields placeholder)
160	
161	## Suggested approach
162	
163	Spend the conversation in three phases, roughly:
164	
165	1. **Stress-test each option** — for each of A, B, C, walk
166	   through one concrete worked example (e.g., a P3 cost
167	   trial) and ask: how is the data accessed for analysis?
168	   How is it browsed? How does cross-trial comparison work?
169	   How does it interact with sanctum reorganization?
170	2. **Pick.** State the choice and the reason. Record as the
171	   conclusion of `c-momaa5o9`.
172	3. **Specify** — write up the storage layout and engine
173	   contracts as a short section in
174	   `packages/laboratory/README.md`, or as a new doc under
175	   `docs/laboratory/`. Don't draft it as a separate spec —
176	   the laboratory's own README is the home.
177	
178	## Click hygiene
179	
180	Conclude `c-momaa5o9` with the decision and the high-level
181	shape (option chosen, layout summary, linkage mechanism).
182	Implementation of the archive engine happens in the
183	implementation handoff — that's the next thread to pick up.
184	
185	## Companion handoff
186	
187	`.scratch/handoff-laboratory-implementation.md` — the engine
188	implementation thread. The archive design decision unblocks
189	its probe + archive steps; that handoff has notes about where
190	this conversation becomes blocking.
191

# Handoff — Laboratory archive design conversation

A focused design conversation to resolve the open archive-target
question (click `c-momaa5o9`). Output is a decision plus enough
specificity that the archive engine and the probe engines can be
implemented without further design pings.

## Where we are

The Laboratory apparatus is built and operable end-to-end with stub
engines:

- Trial writ type, manifest CLI (`nsg lab-trial-post`), and the
  `post-and-collect-default` rig template are wired and live.
- Five phase orchestrators (setup → scenario → probes → archive
  → teardown) graft their per-phase engines from the trial config.
- All work engines are stubs that yield placeholder data; rigs
  crawl through cleanly. Smoke-test verified live in vibers.

Live trial run, for orientation:

    nsg --guild-root /workspace/vibers writ show w-momen904
    nsg --guild-root /workspace/vibers rig show rig-momencrs-bfc587fd

## The decision in one frame

Where do the data the lab CAPTURES live? Three artifact streams:

- **Stacks dump** — the test guild's books exported (writs, links,
  sessions, plus whatever new books). Currently produced by
  `probe-stacks-dump`. Sean's earlier infra spec said
  JSON-per-table.
- **Codex history** — per-commit diffs + a commits-manifest
  between the codex base and head SHAs. Currently produced by
  `probe-git-range`. Sean's earlier spec said git format-patch
  output.
- **Probe metadata + cross-references** — counts, the
  scenario writ id, plugin pin info, etc. Lighter; doesn't have
  the same scale as the dumps.

## The three options

### A. Lab-host guild's books

Captured data lives natively in the lab guild's stacks. New books
register: `lab-trial-archives` (parent record per trial),
`lab-trial-stacks-dumps` (rows from the test guild's books, tagged
by source-book + source-row-id), `lab-trial-codex-commits` (one
row per captured commit, body = the diff).

- **Pro:** the lab guild becomes self-describing — a single
  query surface for all trial data; supports cross-trial
  analysis without filesystem walking.
- **Pro:** the books contract handles schema evolution
  (`books_<plugin>_<book>_<sha>`), retention, indexing.
- **Pro:** survives sanctum reorganization (X-num renumbering,
  experiment migration) without rewriting paths.
- **Con:** schema design work for capture-shaped data —
  particularly the stacks-dump shape, since rows from many books
  with different schemas need a common storage shape.
- **Con:** large diffs as TEXT in SQLite is fine but limits
  scale; would need a blob-extraction policy at some threshold.
- **Con:** loses the filesystem-as-archive intuition the
  original infra spec built around. Browsing trial results means
  going through nsg / Oculus rather than `cd experiments/X...`.

### B. Sanctum mirror

Captured data lands as files under
`experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/artifacts/trial-<NNN>-<variant>/`.
Same shape as the original infrastructure spec (the one this
apparatus supersedes): stacks-export/, codex-history/, run.yaml,
README.md.

- **Pro:** continuity with the existing infra spec — the spec
  text already describes this layout in detail; adoption is
  basically "make the apparatus produce what the bash scripts
  would have."
- **Pro:** browsable. `cd experiments/X016/artifacts/trial-001-…/`
  and you see everything.
- **Pro:** filesystem is the archive. No DB schema migration
  concerns; old trials stay readable as long as the directory
  tree exists.
- **Con:** two homes for what's conceptually one body of data
  (writ in the lab guild's books pointing at filesystem paths).
- **Con:** filesystem-as-database friction — cross-trial
  queries mean walking directories with shell tools or building a
  static index.
- **Con:** the `experiments/X<NNN>-<slug>/` tree was built when
  experiments were patron-authored. With the lab apparatus
  generating runs, that tree's shape may need to evolve.
- **Con:** sanctum and lab-host guild may not be the same
  filesystem — adds a "sanctum path" config to the lab apparatus
  (not necessarily co-located).

### C. Hybrid

Books in the lab guild hold a small per-trial archive record
(metadata + paths/pointers); contents (stacks dump + codex
diffs) live as files in a configured archive root. The trial
writ links to its archive record via a kit-contributed link
kind (`laboratory.archived-as`).

- **Pro:** queryable provenance + filesystem browsability. Best
  of both.
- **Pro:** filesystem can be the sanctum tree (continuity with
  the spec) OR a dedicated archive volume — the path is a
  guild config, not hard-coded.
- **Con:** more moving parts. Archive engine writes both books
  and filesystem; teardown safety check has two surfaces to
  verify.
- **Con:** "where does this trial's data live?" has two
  answers. Tools must know to consult both.

## What the conversation must produce

1. **Pick A, B, or C.**
2. **Storage layout** — the concrete shape:
   - For (A): exact book schemas (lab-trial-archives,
     lab-trial-stacks-dumps, lab-trial-codex-commits — or
     whatever set we settle on).
   - For (B): exact directory layout (does the existing
     `experiments/X<NNN>/artifacts/trial-NNN-<variant>/` shape
     survive verbatim, or does it evolve?).
   - For (C): both schemas + the path-config field on the
     laboratory apparatus.
3. **Trial writ → archive linkage** — how does
   `nsg writ show <trial-id>` surface the archive location?
   Most likely a `laboratory.archived-as` link kind contributed
   to the Clerk; targets are either an archive-record writ id
   (A or C) or an archive path (B; opaque string in the link
   `kind`).
4. **Stacks-dump row shape (if A or C with books)** — the
   test guild's books have heterogeneous schemas. How is one
   row from `clerk/writs` stored alongside one row from
   `animator/sessions`? Most plausible: a `book` + `row` JSON
   blob per dump entry, indexed on `(trialId, sourceBook,
   sourceRowId)`. Worth confirming.
5. **Probe engine output contract** — once we know where
   archives go, the probe yields shape is fixed. Probes yield
   either:
   - paths (`{ files: [{ path, size, sha }] }`) → archive
     engine packages them
   - book row counts + ids (`{ written: { writs: 12, … } }`) →
     archive engine consolidates
   - both, depending on probe.

## Pointers to read first

- `nsg click extract --id c-momaa5o9` — the archive click
- `nsg click extract --id c-mom18vmh` — full apparatus design
  context
- `experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md` — the
  earlier standalone-bash spec; its filesystem layout is the
  starting point for option B
- `packages/laboratory/src/types.ts` — current
  `TrialArchiveDecl` shape (provisional)
- `packages/laboratory/src/engines/stubs.ts` — what `lab.archive`
  currently does (logs + yields placeholder)

## Suggested approach

Spend the conversation in three phases, roughly:

1. **Stress-test each option** — for each of A, B, C, walk
   through one concrete worked example (e.g., a P3 cost
   trial) and ask: how is the data accessed for analysis?
   How is it browsed? How does cross-trial comparison work?
   How does it interact with sanctum reorganization?
2. **Pick.** State the choice and the reason. Record as the
   conclusion of `c-momaa5o9`.
3. **Specify** — write up the storage layout and engine
   contracts as a short section in
   `packages/laboratory/README.md`, or as a new doc under
   `docs/laboratory/`. Don't draft it as a separate spec —
   the laboratory's own README is the home.

## Click hygiene

Conclude `c-momaa5o9` with the decision and the high-level
shape (option chosen, layout summary, linkage mechanism).
Implementation of the archive engine happens in the
implementation handoff — that's the next thread to pick up.

## Companion handoff

`.scratch/handoff-laboratory-implementation.md` — the engine
implementation thread. The archive design decision unblocks
its probe + archive steps; that handoff has notes about where
this conversation becomes blocking.

1	/**
2	 * Skeleton (stub) engine implementations.
3	 *
4	 * Every Laboratory engine other than `lab.orchestrate` is currently a
5	 * no-op stub: it logs to console, yields a `{ skipped: true, ... }`
6	 * payload, and returns success. Stubs exist so the rig template wires
7	 * end-to-end — a posted trial crawls cleanly through orchestrate →
8	 * grafted-stubs → completion — well before any engine does real work.
9	 *
10	 * Each stub will be replaced by its real implementation under the
11	 * matching click:
12	 *
13	 *   - lab.codex-setup / lab.codex-teardown          — c-moma9y1k
14	 *   - lab.guild-setup / lab.guild-teardown          — c-momaa03d
15	 *   - lab.commission-post-xguild
16	 *     lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild             — c-momaa1vt
17	 *   - lab.probe-stacks-dump
18	 *     lab.probe-git-range                            — c-momaa3w7
19	 *   - lab.archive                                    — c-momaa5o9
20	 *
21	 * When swapping a stub for its real implementation, move it to its
22	 * own file under `engines/`; `stubs.ts` shrinks toward empty.
23	 */
24	
25	import type { EngineDesign, EngineRunResult } from '@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus';
26	
27	/** Build a stub engine: logs, yields a placeholder, returns completed. */
28	function stubEngine(designId: string, summary: string): EngineDesign {
29	  return {
30	    id: designId,
31	    async run(givens, context): Promise<EngineRunResult> {
32	      // eslint-disable-next-line no-console
33	      console.log(
34	        `[${designId}] STUB invoked (rig=${context.rigId} engine=${context.engineId}); ` +
35	          `givens keys=[${Object.keys(givens).join(', ')}]; summary="${summary}"`,
36	      );
37	      return {
38	        status: 'completed',
39	        yields: {
40	          stub: true,
41	          designId,
42	          summary,
43	          // Echo a minimal subset of givens so downstream stubs can
44	          // resolve `${yields.<id>.<key>}` templates without crashing.
45	          givenKeys: Object.keys(givens),
46	        },
47	      };
48	    },
49	  };
50	}
51	
52	// ── Fixture engines ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
53	
54	export const codexSetupStub = stubEngine(
55	  'lab.codex-setup',
56	  'clone upstream codex at base SHA, push to fresh GH repo, register via nsg codex add',
57	);
58	
59	export const codexTeardownStub = stubEngine(
60	  'lab.codex-teardown',
61	  'gh repo delete the codex repo created by lab.codex-setup',
62	);
63	
64	export const guildSetupStub = stubEngine(
65	  'lab.guild-setup',
66	  'nsg init + per-pin nsg plugin install + nsg codex add + deep-merge guild.json + copy files',
67	);
68	
69	export const guildTeardownStub = stubEngine(
70	  'lab.guild-teardown',
71	  'rm -rf the test guild dir created by lab.guild-setup',
72	);
73	
74	// ── Scenario engines ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
75	
76	export const commissionPostXguildStub = stubEngine(
77	  'lab.commission-post-xguild',
78	  'shell out to nsg commission-post --guild-root <test-guild> --brief <path>',
79	);
80	
81	export const waitForWritTerminalXguildStub = stubEngine(
82	  'lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild',
83	  'poll writ status across guilds via nsg --guild-root <other> writ show',
84	);
85	
86	// ── Probe engines ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
87	
88	export const probeStacksDumpStub = stubEngine(
89	  'lab.probe-stacks-dump',
90	  'export every book in test guild as JSON-per-table with carve-out for >50KB rows',
91	);
92	
93	export const probeGitRangeStub = stubEngine(
94	  'lab.probe-git-range',
95	  'capture per-commit diffs + commits-manifest.yaml between base and head SHAs',
96	);
97	
98	// ── Archive engine ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
99	
100	export const archiveStub = stubEngine(
101	  'lab.archive',
102	  'archive probe outputs to the configured target (open: lab books vs sanctum vs hybrid; c-momaa5o9)',
103	);
104

1	/**
2	 * The Laboratory — public types.
3	 *
4	 * Two concerns live here:
5	 *
6	 *   1. The `trial` writ type configuration — its state machine, registered
7	 *      with the Clerk at startup. Mirrors the mandate lifecycle (`new →
8	 *      open → completed | failed | cancelled`, with `stuck` as a
9	 *      non-terminal "needs attention" state off `open`). Trials are leaves
10	 *      in v1 — the `experiment` parent type is reserved for v2 (see click
11	 *      c-momaacry).
12	 *
13	 *   2. The `ext.laboratory.config` schema — the structured trial
14	 *      configuration carried on every trial writ. Mirrors the YAML
15	 *      manifest shape exactly so the CLI's translation layer is a thin
16	 *      validator + post step.
17	 *
18	 * The legacy `LaboratoryConfig` (data-mirroring stub) is removed — that
19	 * plugin's no-op phase is over.
20	 *
21	 * See: experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md (the standalone-bash
22	 * spec the apparatus supersedes; its run.yaml shape is the template here).
23	 */
24	
25	import type { WritTypeConfig } from '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus';
26	
27	// ── Writ-type configuration ──────────────────────────────────────────
28	
29	/** The writ-type name registered with the Clerk. */
30	export const TRIAL_TYPE_NAME = 'trial';
31	
32	/**
33	 * The `trial` writ type. State machine mirrors mandate's
34	 * `new → open → completed/failed/cancelled` with `stuck` as a
35	 * non-terminal off `open`. Children-cascade behavior is included so
36	 * trials can be nested under a future `experiment` parent without a
37	 * config-level migration; in v1 trials are leaves and the cascade
38	 * triggers are inert.
39	 */
40	export const TRIAL_WRIT_TYPE_CONFIG: WritTypeConfig = {
41	  name: TRIAL_TYPE_NAME,
42	  states: [
43	    {
44	      name: 'new',
45	      classification: 'initial',
46	      allowedTransitions: ['open', 'cancelled'],
47	    },
48	    {
49	      name: 'open',
50	      classification: 'active',
51	      allowedTransitions: ['stuck', 'completed', 'failed', 'cancelled'],
52	    },
53	    {
54	      name: 'stuck',
55	      classification: 'active',
56	      attrs: ['stuck'],
57	      allowedTransitions: ['open', 'failed', 'cancelled'],
58	    },
59	    {
60	      name: 'completed',
61	      classification: 'terminal',
62	      attrs: ['success'],
63	      allowedTransitions: [],
64	    },
65	    {
66	      name: 'failed',
67	      classification: 'terminal',
68	      attrs: ['failure'],
69	      allowedTransitions: [],
70	    },
71	    {
72	      name: 'cancelled',
73	      classification: 'terminal',
74	      attrs: ['cancelled'],
75	      allowedTransitions: [],
76	    },
77	  ],
78	  childrenBehavior: {
79	    allSuccess: { transition: 'completed', copyResolution: true },
80	    anyFailure: { transition: 'failed', copyResolution: true },
81	    parentTerminal: {
82	      transition: 'cancelled',
83	      resolution: 'Automatically cancelled due to parent termination',
84	    },
85	  },
86	};
87	
88	// ── ext.laboratory.config schema ─────────────────────────────────────
89	//
90	// The structured trial configuration. Mirrors the YAML manifest shape
91	// (see docs/laboratory/manifest-format.md once authored). Every field
92	// here will be validated by the manifest CLI before the trial writ is
93	// posted.
94	
95	/**
96	 * Slug carried on every trial — used in disposable-surface naming
97	 * (codex repo, guild dir). Authored by the manifest. Length-limited at
98	 * the CLI boundary (see manifest-cli child); engines treat it as
99	 * already-validated input.
100	 *
101	 * Format target: lowercase kebab-case, alphanumeric + hyphen only,
102	 * 1–40 chars. Disposable-resource names of the shape
103	 * `trial-<slug>-<trialId-prefix>` slot below 63-char DNS / GH-repo
104	 * limits with margin.
105	 */
106	export type TrialSlug = string;
107	
108	/**
109	 * One fixture declaration in a trial. Fixtures handle setup and
110	 * teardown of disposable surfaces (codex repos, test guilds, etc.)
111	 * and form a dependency DAG.
112	 *
113	 * Setup and teardown are paired engines. By convention the teardown
114	 * engine id is the setup id with `-setup` swapped for `-teardown`
115	 * (e.g. `lab.codex-setup` ↔ `lab.codex-teardown`). The `teardownEngineId`
116	 * field overrides this default when the convention doesn't fit.
117	 *
118	 * MVP0 ships trial-scope only — every fixture is fresh per trial,
119	 * torn down at trial end. The `scope` and `mutability` hooks are
120	 * reserved for v2 (see click c-mom19282).
121	 */
122	export interface TrialFixtureDecl {
123	  /** Fixture id within this trial. Referenced by `dependsOn`. */
124	  id: string;
125	  /** Setup engine design id (e.g. `'lab.codex-setup'`). */
126	  engineId: string;
127	  /** Override teardown engine id. Defaults to convention (`-setup` → `-teardown`). */
128	  teardownEngineId?: string;
129	  /** Givens passed to BOTH the setup and teardown engines. */
130	  givens: Record<string, unknown>;
131	  /** Other fixture ids that must be set up before this one. */
132	  dependsOn?: string[];
133	  /** Reserved for v2 — only `'trial'` valid in v1. */
134	  scope?: 'trial';
135	  /** Reserved for v2 — informational in v1. */
136	  mutability?: 'mutable' | 'read-only' | 'snapshotted';
137	}
138	
139	/**
140	 * The scenario — what work runs inside the trial after fixtures are
141	 * up. In v1, scenarios are themselves engine designs (commission-post-
142	 * xguild + wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild are the canonical pair).
143	 * Future scenarios may compose multiple engine invocations.
144	 */
145	export interface TrialScenarioDecl {
146	  /** Engine design id to run as the scenario. */
147	  engineId: string;
148	  /** Givens passed to the scenario engine. */
149	  givens: Record<string, unknown>;
150	}
151	
152	/** A probe — extracts data from one or more fixtures. */
153	export interface TrialProbeDecl {
154	  /** Probe id within this trial. */
155	  id: string;
156	  /** Engine design id (e.g. `'lab.probe-stacks-dump'`). */
157	  engineId: string;
158	  /** Givens passed to the probe engine. */
159	  givens: Record<string, unknown>;
160	}
161	
162	/**
163	 * The archive declaration — where probe outputs land at trial end.
164	 * The engine and storage layout are being designed under click
165	 * c-momaa5o9; this shape is provisional.
166	 */
167	export interface TrialArchiveDecl {
168	  /** Engine design id (e.g. `'lab.archive'`). */
169	  engineId: string;
170	  /** Givens passed to the archive engine. */
171	  givens: Record<string, unknown>;
172	}
173	
174	/**
175	 * The structured payload carried on every trial writ at
176	 * `ext.laboratory.config`. The manifest YAML on disk has the same
177	 * shape plus a top-level `description` field that lands on the writ
178	 * body instead of in `ext`.
179	 */
180	export interface LaboratoryTrialConfig {
181	  /** Trial slug (used in disposable-surface naming). */
182	  slug: TrialSlug;
183	  /** Fixtures to set up (DAG; topo-sort happens at template time). */
184	  fixtures: TrialFixtureDecl[];
185	  /** The workload that runs after setup. */
186	  scenario: TrialScenarioDecl;
187	  /** Probes to extract data. Run after the scenario reaches its end-condition. */
188	  probes: TrialProbeDecl[];
189	  /** Archive — where probe outputs go. */
190	  archive: TrialArchiveDecl;
191	}
192	
193	// ── GuildConfig augmentation ─────────────────────────────────────────
194	//
195	// The Laboratory contributes no top-level guild.json config in v1.
196	// Trial-specific configuration lives on individual writs at
197	// `ext.laboratory.config`, not in guild config. Reserved for future
198	// global lab settings (e.g. default codex org, archive root path).
199	
200	export interface LaboratoryConfig {
201	  /**
202	   * Reserved for v2. No fields read in v1 — present so guild.json
203	   * fragments that include an empty `laboratory: {}` block still
204	   * typecheck.
205	   */
206	  reserved?: never;
207	}
208	
209	declare module '@shardworks/nexus-core' {
210	  interface GuildConfig {
211	    laboratory?: LaboratoryConfig;
212	  }
213	}
214

1	/**
2	 * @shardworks/laboratory-apparatus — The Laboratory.
3	 *
4	 * Apparatus for running trial-shaped experiments on guild
5	 * configurations. Audiences:
6	 *
7	 *   - **Nexus dev:** cost/quality tuning (P3 work, prompt evaluation,
8	 *     plugin variant comparison) — automate the experimental-
9	 *     infrastructure spec at experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-
10	 *     artifacts.md.
11	 *   - **End users:** evaluate their own prompts/plugins/configs by
12	 *     authoring trial manifests against a stable apparatus surface.
13	 *
14	 * Architecture (MVP0):
15	 *
16	 *   - One writ type: `trial` — a single execution unit. Lifecycle
17	 *     mirrors mandate. (Higher-level `experiment` grouping is parked
18	 *     for v2; click c-momaacry.)
19	 *   - One canonical rig template: `post-and-collect-default` —
20	 *     composes fixture-setup, scenario, probes, teardown, and archive
21	 *     engines from the writ's `ext.laboratory.config`.
22	 *   - Engines: fixtures (setup/teardown, DAG), scenario (cross-guild
23	 *     commission post + wait), probes (data extraction), archive
24	 *     (still in design at click c-momaa5o9).
25	 *
26	 * The plugin is currently a skeleton — only the trial writ-type
27	 * registration is wired. Engine designs, rig templates, and CLI tools
28	 * are added as their respective implementation children land.
29	 *
30	 * See: experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md
31	 */
32	
33	import type { Plugin } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
34	import { guild } from '@shardworks/nexus-core';
35	import type { ClerkApi } from '@shardworks/clerk-apparatus';
36	
37	// ── Public type re-exports ───────────────────────────────────────────
38	
39	export {
40	  TRIAL_TYPE_NAME,
41	  TRIAL_WRIT_TYPE_CONFIG,
42	} from './types.ts';
43	export type {
44	  LaboratoryConfig,
45	  LaboratoryTrialConfig,
46	  TrialFixtureDecl,
47	  TrialScenarioDecl,
48	  TrialProbeDecl,
49	  TrialArchiveDecl,
50	  TrialSlug,
51	} from './types.ts';
52	
53	// ── Apparatus ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
54	
55	import { TRIAL_WRIT_TYPE_CONFIG } from './types.ts';
56	import { engines } from './engines/index.ts';
57	import { rigTemplates, rigTemplateMappings } from './template.ts';
58	import { tools } from './tools/index.ts';
59	
60	const laboratoryPlugin: Plugin = {
61	  apparatus: {
62	    /**
63	     * Hard requires — apparatuses whose APIs the Laboratory's startup
64	     * code calls directly (clerk for writ-type registration), plus
65	     * those whose presence is structurally needed for the lab's
66	     * contributions to be useful (spider for rig-template execution,
67	     * fabricator for engine-design registration). Stacks is required
68	     * transitively via clerk.
69	     */
70	    requires: ['stacks', 'clerk', 'spider', 'fabricator'],
71	
72	    /**
73	     * Recommended at startup; required at engine-execution time when
74	     * the relevant fixture or scenario engine actually fires. Marking
75	     * them as recommends (not requires) lets a guild start the lab
76	     * without these installed — the trials that touch the missing
77	     * surface fail when their engine runs, not at startup.
78	     */
79	    recommends: ['codexes', 'animator'],
80	
81	    /**
82	     * Kit contributions wired up:
83	     *
84	     *   - `engines`              — every Laboratory engine design
85	     *                               (5 phase orchestrators + the
86	     *                               work-engine stubs that get
87	     *                               grafted by them). Stubs land
88	     *                               their real behavior under their
89	     *                               respective implementation clicks.
90	     *   - `rigTemplates`         — `post-and-collect-default` —
91	     *                               enumerates the five phase
92	     *                               orchestrators in sequence.
93	     *   - `rigTemplateMappings`  — `trial` → `post-and-collect-default`.
94	     *   - `tools`                — the manifest CLI surface
95	     *                               (`nsg lab trial-post`).
96	     */
97	    supportKit: {
98	      engines,
99	      rigTemplates,
100	      rigTemplateMappings,
101	      tools,
102	    },
103	
104	    async start() {
105	      // Register the `trial` writ type with the Clerk. The
106	      // registration window closes at framework `phase:started`;
107	      // calling later throws.
108	      const clerk = guild().apparatus<ClerkApi>('clerk');
109	      clerk.registerWritType(TRIAL_WRIT_TYPE_CONFIG);
110	    },
111	  },
112	};
113	
114	export default laboratoryPlugin;
115

1	/**
2	 * Engine bag — every engine the Laboratory contributes via supportKit.
3	 *
4	 * Keys are the engine design ids the Spider's template references.
5	 * The Fabricator scans this object at startup and registers each
6	 * design under the laboratory plugin id.
7	 *
8	 * The five phase orchestrators (`lab.{setup,scenario,probes,archive,
9	 * teardown}-phase`) form the rig template's static backbone; they
10	 * read `writ.ext.laboratory.config` at run time and emit per-phase
11	 * grafts. The other engines (codex/guild fixtures, scenario,
12	 * probes, archive) are the work engines those grafts reference.
13	 *
14	 * Stubs land their real implementations under their respective
15	 * implementation children.
16	 */
17	
18	import type { EngineDesign } from '@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus';
19	import { phaseEngines } from './phases.ts';
20	import {
21	  archiveStub,
22	  codexSetupStub,
23	  codexTeardownStub,
24	  commissionPostXguildStub,
25	  guildSetupStub,
26	  guildTeardownStub,
27	  probeGitRangeStub,
28	  probeStacksDumpStub,
29	  waitForWritTerminalXguildStub,
30	} from './stubs.ts';
31	
32	export const engines: Record<string, EngineDesign> = {
33	  // Phase orchestrators (template backbone)
34	  ...phaseEngines,
35	
36	  // Work engines (graft targets)
37	  'lab.codex-setup': codexSetupStub,
38	  'lab.codex-teardown': codexTeardownStub,
39	  'lab.guild-setup': guildSetupStub,
40	  'lab.guild-teardown': guildTeardownStub,
41	  'lab.commission-post-xguild': commissionPostXguildStub,
42	  'lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild': waitForWritTerminalXguildStub,
43	  'lab.probe-stacks-dump': probeStacksDumpStub,
44	  'lab.probe-git-range': probeGitRangeStub,
45	  'lab.archive': archiveStub,
46	};
47

/**
 * Engine bag — every engine the Laboratory contributes via supportKit.
 *
 * Keys are the engine design ids the Spider's template references.
 * The Fabricator scans this object at startup and registers each
 * design under the laboratory plugin id.
 *
 * The five phase orchestrators (`lab.{setup,scenario,probes,archive,
 * teardown}-phase`) form the rig template's static backbone; they
 * read `writ.ext.laboratory.config` at run time and emit per-phase
 * grafts. The other engines (codex/guild fixtures, scenario,
 * probes, archive) are the work engines those grafts reference.
 *
 * Stubs land their real implementations under their respective
 * implementation children.
 */

import type { EngineDesign } from '@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus';
import { phaseEngines } from './phases.ts';
import {
  archiveStub,
  codexSetupStub,
  codexTeardownStub,
  commissionPostXguildStub,
  guildSetupStub,
  guildTeardownStub,
  probeGitRangeStub,
  probeStacksDumpStub,
  waitForWritTerminalXguildStub,
} from './stubs.ts';

export const engines: Record<string, EngineDesign> = {
  // Phase orchestrators (template backbone)
  ...phaseEngines,

  // Work engines (graft targets)
  'lab.codex-setup': codexSetupStub,
  'lab.codex-teardown': codexTeardownStub,
  'lab.guild-setup': guildSetupStub,
  'lab.guild-teardown': guildTeardownStub,
  'lab.commission-post-xguild': commissionPostXguildStub,
  'lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild': waitForWritTerminalXguildStub,
  'lab.probe-stacks-dump': probeStacksDumpStub,
  'lab.probe-git-range': probeGitRangeStub,
  'lab.archive': archiveStub,
};

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * The Laboratory's rig templates.
3	 *
4	 * MVP0 ships ONE template: `post-and-collect-default`. Its engine
5	 * list IS the trial-flow backbone — five phase orchestrators
6	 * (setup → scenario → probes → archive → teardown) wired in
7	 * sequence. Each phase orchestrator is a clockwork engine: it
8	 * reads `writ.ext.laboratory.config`, computes the per-phase
9	 * graft, and returns immediately. The grafted engines do the
10	 * real work; the template documents the flow.
11	 *
12	 * Why the backbone is in the template (not in a single
13	 * orchestrator):
14	 *
15	 *   - The template is the user-facing shape document for a trial.
16	 *     With phases visible, reading the template tells you "this
17	 *     rig runs setup, then scenario, then probes, then archive,
18	 *     then teardown."
19	 *   - Per-phase failure visibility: oculus and introspection tools
20	 *     see the phase backbone in the rig's engine list; "the probes
21	 *     phase failed" is a more useful signal than "the orchestrator
22	 *     failed somewhere."
23	 *   - Each phase orchestrator implementation is cohesive (DAG
24	 *     sort, parallel scatter, reverse-sequential chain) — splitting
25	 *     them avoids one big function with phase-mode flags.
26	 *   - Sets up an extension point: a future plugin contributing
27	 *     `lab.warmup-phase` (or similar) could slot into a custom
28	 *     template alongside the existing phases.
29	 *
30	 * Cross-phase data flow happens at the work-engine layer, not the
31	 * orchestrator layer. All grafted engines share a single namespace,
32	 * so e.g. the scenario engine's upstream points directly at each
33	 * fixture-setup engine — phase orchestrators don't need to relay
34	 * yields between phases.
35	 *
36	 * Rig template name vs writ-type mapping: the Spider qualifies
37	 * kit-contributed template keys by plugin id when storing them in
38	 * its registry. The unqualified key `'post-and-collect-default'`
39	 * registers as `'laboratory.post-and-collect-default'`. The mapping
40	 * value below uses the unqualified form because spider.ts joins
41	 * the plugin id when resolving mappings (matches astrolabe's
42	 * convention).
43	 */
44	
45	import type { RigTemplate } from '@shardworks/spider-apparatus';
46	
47	/**
48	 * The post-and-collect-default rig template — five phase
49	 * orchestrators in sequence.
50	 *
51	 * Each phase orchestrator reads the trial's `ext.laboratory.config`,
52	 * computes its phase's graft, and returns. graftTail is set only
53	 * on `lab.teardown-phase` (the rig's true end) — intermediate
54	 * phases let the next phase fire immediately, since real
55	 * work-engines wait on explicit upstream refs.
56	 */
57	export const POST_AND_COLLECT_DEFAULT: RigTemplate = {
58	  engines: [
59	    {
60	      id: 'setup-phase',
61	      designId: 'lab.setup-phase',
62	      // Head engine — no upstream.
63	      givens: { writ: '${writ}' },
64	    },
65	    {
66	      id: 'scenario-phase',
67	      designId: 'lab.scenario-phase',
68	      upstream: ['setup-phase'],
69	      givens: { writ: '${writ}' },
70	    },
71	    {
72	      id: 'probes-phase',
73	      designId: 'lab.probes-phase',
74	      upstream: ['scenario-phase'],
75	      givens: { writ: '${writ}' },
76	    },
77	    {
78	      id: 'archive-phase',
79	      designId: 'lab.archive-phase',
80	      upstream: ['probes-phase'],
81	      givens: { writ: '${writ}' },
82	    },
83	    {
84	      id: 'teardown-phase',
85	      designId: 'lab.teardown-phase',
86	      upstream: ['archive-phase'],
87	      givens: { writ: '${writ}' },
88	    },
89	  ],
90	  // resolutionEngine intentionally unset — the Spider falls back
91	  //   to the last completed engine, which is the final teardown
92	  //   (or archive when there are no fixtures), via the
93	  //   teardown-phase orchestrator's graftTail.
94	};
95	
96	/** The Laboratory's rig templates, keyed by unqualified template name. */
97	export const rigTemplates: Record<string, RigTemplate> = {
98	  'post-and-collect-default': POST_AND_COLLECT_DEFAULT,
99	};
100	
101	/** Writ-type → unqualified-template-name mappings. */
102	export const rigTemplateMappings: Record<string, string> = {
103	  trial: 'post-and-collect-default',
104	};
105
