content.content:
1	# Laboratory apparatus — framework recon
2	
3	Findings from reading the live framework source (the live `/workspace/nexus`
4	monorepo, on whatever SHA `nsg` is currently dispatching to). Maps each
5	prerequisite the apparatus design depends on to what the framework actually
6	offers, and surfaces gaps.
7	
8	## TL;DR
9	
10	All the load-bearing prerequisites are satisfied:
11	
12	- **Non-anima engines: yes.** The Fabricator's `EngineDesign.run()` returns
13	  `{ status: 'completed', yields }` for synchronous work — exactly the shape
14	  we need for fixture/probe/scenario engines. Real precedent exists
15	  (`spider.seal` does git ops via Scriptorium and returns completed inline).
16	- **Plugin install accepts SHAs: yes** — `nsg plugin install` shells out to
17	  `npm install --save <source>`, which natively handles git+URL refs (and
18	  `#<sha>` pins on those URLs). The framework code passes the source string
19	  through unchanged.
20	- **Writ types via kit contributions: yes** — `ClerkApi.registerWritType`
21	  is called from the plugin's `apparatus.start()`. The Astrolabe plugin
22	  registers two custom types (`step`, `observation-set`) this way.
23	- **Rig templates via kit contributions: yes** — `SpiderKit.rigTemplates`
24	  + `SpiderKit.rigTemplateMappings` carry plugin-contributed templates and
25	  writ-type mappings.
26	- **Engine designs via kit contributions: yes** — `kit.engines: Record<id,
27	  EngineDesign>` is the standard shape; the Fabricator scans every kit's
28	  `engines` field and registers it under the contributing plugin's id.
29	- **Plugin CLI tools: yes** — tools registered via `supportKit.tools`
30	  auto-discover into `nsg <tool-name>` (with auto-grouping when names
31	  share a hyphen prefix). `nsg lab trial post` would come from a tool
32	  named `lab-trial-post`.
33	- **gh org permissions: yellow.** Org membership active; token scope is
34	  `repo`. Repo-create probably works (member-permitted in most org
35	  policies); repo-delete may not — admin perms needed depending on org
36	  policy. Need to verify by attempting once before relying on it.
37	
38	The single piece the apparatus design got wrong is `nsg codex add` —
39	**it does NOT take a base SHA.** It only takes `(name, remoteUrl)`. The
40	SHA pinning happens earlier in the flow: the run-init script clones
41	upstream at the base SHA, pushes that as a fresh GH repo, then `nsg codex
42	add <new-repo>` clones the fresh repo at HEAD (which IS the base SHA by
43	construction). The original spec already describes this flow correctly;
44	the misread was about which step does the pinning.
45	
46	No design adjustments required from the recon.
47	
48	## Detailed findings
49	
50	### 1. `nsg init`
51	
52	Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/commands/init.ts`
53	
54	Surface: `nsg init <path> [--name <name>] [--model <model>]`
55	
56	- Creates `<path>/`, `<path>/.nexus/`, `<path>/guild.json`,
57	  `<path>/package.json`, `<path>/.gitignore`.
58	- Refuses if `<path>` exists and is non-empty.
59	- Does NOT git init.
60	- Does NOT install plugins (separate step).
61	- Default model is `sonnet`.
62	- When framework is published (`VERSION !== '0.0.0'`), pins
63	  `@shardworks/nexus` in deps and runs `npm install`. In dev (link
64	  mode, VERSION = '0.0.0'), no install.
65	
66	Gap vs. apparatus design: none. Spec already calls `nsg init <guild-dir>`
67	and follows up with separate `plugin install` / `codex add` steps.
68	
69	### 2. `nsg plugin install`
70	
71	Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/commands/plugin.ts`
72	
73	Surface: `nsg plugin install <source> [--type registry|link]`
74	
75	Handler logic:
76	- If source is a path that exists and is a directory → link mode
77	  (`npm install --save file:<dir>` or `pnpm add link:<dir>`).
78	- Otherwise → registry mode (`npm install --save <source>`).
79	
80	The `<source>` string is passed unchanged to npm. So:
81	- `@shardworks/foo@1.2.3` — semver works
82	- `@shardworks/foo@latest` — dist-tag works
83	- `git+https://github.com/foo/bar#<sha>` — SHA pin works (npm-native)
84	- `github:foo/bar#<sha>` — short-form git ref works
85	- `./local/path` — local link works
86	
87	Plugin-id derivation: the framework parses the package name out of the
88	source string; for git URLs it falls back to reading the most recently
89	added entry in the guild's `package.json` after install (relies on
90	key insertion order — explicitly noted in code as a known compromise).
91	
92	Gap vs. apparatus design: none. The handoff prompt's Q "does install
93	accept git SHAs" is yes, via standard npm git+URL syntax.
94	
95	### 3. `nsg codex add`
96	
97	Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/codexes/src/tools/codex-add.ts`
98	
99	Surface: `nsg codex add --name <name> --remote-url <url>` (the auto-
100	grouping makes positional flags work too).
101	
102	Handler logic: clones a bare copy to `.nexus/codexes/<name>.git`, registers
103	`{ remoteUrl }` under `codexes.registered[name]` in guild.json. **No SHA
104	parameter.** The clone is bare-default (HEAD is whatever the remote points
105	at).
106	
107	Gap vs. apparatus design: the spec implied `nsg codex add <repo-url> --sha
108	<codex-base-sha>`; that flag does not exist. The fix is *order*, not
109	behavior: clone upstream at base SHA → push to fresh repo → `nsg codex add`
110	the fresh repo (which is at base SHA by construction). The spec text
111	"`gh repo create ... --source=<temp-dir> --push`" already describes this
112	correctly; only the example invocation `nsg codex add <repo-url> --sha
113	<codex-base-sha>` was misleading.
114	
115	### 4. Engine designs (the load-bearing question)
116	
117	Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.ts`
118	
119	```ts
120	export interface EngineDesign {
121	  id: string;
122	  run(givens, context): Promise<EngineRunResult>;
123	  collect?(sessionId, givens, context): Promise<unknown>;
124	  retry?: EngineRetryConfig;
125	}
126	
127	export type EngineRunResult =
128	  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown }
129	  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string }
130	  | { status: 'blocked'; blockType: string; condition: unknown; message?: string };
131	```
132	
133	The `'completed'` branch is exactly what shell/script-style engines need.
134	No anima, no session, no Loom involvement — the engine does its work
135	synchronously in `run()` and returns yields directly. The Spider treats
136	the result as `engine-completed` and moves on.
137	
138	Concrete precedent: `spider.seal` (in
139	`/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/seal.ts`) calls
140	`scriptorium.seal()` synchronously and returns
141	`{ status: 'completed', yields }`. Multiple Astrolabe engines do the
142	same (`plan-init`, `plan-finalize`, `inventory-check`, etc.).
143	
144	Implication for the lab: every fixture, probe, and scenario engine I'll
145	write is a sync function that does shell/git/network/fs work and returns
146	yields. No new framework primitive needed.
147	
148	Engines are registered via the plugin's `supportKit.engines: Record<id,
149	EngineDesign>`. The Fabricator scans every kit/supportKit's `engines`
150	field at startup. Two kits contributing the same id throws.
151	
152	### 5. Writ-type registration
153	
154	Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts`
155	
156	`ClerkApi.registerWritType(config: WritTypeConfig)` is called from the
157	plugin's `apparatus.start()`. The registration window closes at
158	`phase:started`; calling after that throws.
159	
160	Astrolabe registers `step` and `observation-set` this way. The Laboratory
161	will register `trial`. The `WritTypeConfig` shape lives in
162	`writ-type-config.ts` (state machine, child behavior, etc.).
163	
164	### 6. Rig templates and mappings
165	
166	Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts`
167	
168	```ts
169	export interface SpiderKit {
170	  rigTemplates?: Record<string, RigTemplate>;
171	  rigTemplateMappings?: Record<string, string>;
172	}
173	```
174	
175	Templates are keyed unqualified; the Spider registers them as
176	`<pluginId>.<key>`. Mappings map writ types to template names.
177	
178	The Laboratory's `supportKit.rigTemplates` will carry
179	`'post-and-collect-default'`; mappings will route `trial` to
180	`'laboratory.post-and-collect-default'` (or whatever the resolved name is —
181	need to check whether mapping values use qualified or unqualified ids).
182	
183	### 7. Plugin CLI tools
184	
185	Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/program.ts`
186	
187	CLI surface for plugin-contributed tools: registered via the `tools`
188	apparatus (kit contribution `tools` field). The CLI auto-discovers and
189	auto-groups by hyphen prefix — `lab-trial-post`, `lab-trial-show`,
190	`lab-trial-list` would group under `nsg lab` as `trial-post`, etc. (need
191	to verify the exact grouping behavior — there's a `findGroupPrefixes`
192	function I didn't read yet).
193	
194	### 8. gh permissions
195	
196	Verified directly:
197	- Org membership in `shardworks`: active (role: member, direct).
198	- Token scopes: `gist`, `read:org`, `repo`. The `repo` scope is
199	  sufficient for `gh repo create` and `gh repo delete` IF the org policy
200	  permits.
201	- Permissions on `shardworks/nexus-mk2`: `{maintain: true, push: true,
202	  triage: true, pull: true, admin: false}`. Admin would simplify
203	  arbitrary repo mgmt; without it we depend on org-level repo policy.
204	
205	What I haven't verified:
206	- Whether org members can create new repos in `shardworks` org. (Would
207	  need an org settings read or a test create.)
208	- Whether org members can delete repos they created. Default GitHub
209	  behavior is yes (creator can always delete), but it's worth confirming
210	  with a test once.
211	
212	Recommendation: keep this as a yellow flag in the apparatus design.
213	Test it on the smoke run by attempting a real codex create + delete; if
214	it fails, surface to Sean and either bump token scopes / org perms or
215	adjust the apparatus to use a different repo namespace.
216	
217	## What this changes about the apparatus design
218	
219	Nothing fundamental. The design is implementable as written. Two minor
220	adjustments:
221	
222	1. **`nsg codex add` invocation:** drop the imagined `--sha` flag. The
223	   codex-add step pins via the repo's HEAD (which is the base SHA after
224	   the gh repo create + push). The original spec text already describes
225	   this correctly; only the literal example invocation was wrong.
226	
227	2. **Codex-fixture engine flow:** Make explicit that the engine must do
228	   the clone+push+create work BEFORE calling `nsg codex add`. Three
229	   substeps inside one engine, not three separate engines.
230	
231	## Open questions for Sean
232	
233	These are things the recon didn't decide for me; quick answers help shape
234	the implementation:
235	
236	1. **Codex repo namespace.** Spec says "same GitHub org as the upstream
237	   codex" (`shardworks` for the dev case). Confirm that's the answer for
238	   v1, not e.g. a personal account or a dedicated `shardworks-experiments`
239	   org.
240	
241	2. **Codex repo visibility.** Spec says `--private`. Confirm.
242	
243	3. **Codex naming format.** Spec says
244	   `experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>`. With trial as the
245	   primary writ type now (no separate experiment), names will be
246	   `trial-<trialId-or-slug>` or similar. Need to pick the actual format
247	   — and decide whether the "experiment" concept survives in the slug
248	   even though it doesn't have a writ type yet.
249	
250	4. **Archive target (the still-open design question).** Three options:
251	   lab-host guild's books, sanctum mirror, or hybrid. Not blocked by
252	   recon — needs a separate conversation.
253

# Laboratory apparatus — framework recon

Findings from reading the live framework source (the live `/workspace/nexus`
monorepo, on whatever SHA `nsg` is currently dispatching to). Maps each
prerequisite the apparatus design depends on to what the framework actually
offers, and surfaces gaps.

## TL;DR

All the load-bearing prerequisites are satisfied:

- **Non-anima engines: yes.** The Fabricator's `EngineDesign.run()` returns
  `{ status: 'completed', yields }` for synchronous work — exactly the shape
  we need for fixture/probe/scenario engines. Real precedent exists
  (`spider.seal` does git ops via Scriptorium and returns completed inline).
- **Plugin install accepts SHAs: yes** — `nsg plugin install` shells out to
  `npm install --save <source>`, which natively handles git+URL refs (and
  `#<sha>` pins on those URLs). The framework code passes the source string
  through unchanged.
- **Writ types via kit contributions: yes** — `ClerkApi.registerWritType`
  is called from the plugin's `apparatus.start()`. The Astrolabe plugin
  registers two custom types (`step`, `observation-set`) this way.
- **Rig templates via kit contributions: yes** — `SpiderKit.rigTemplates`
  + `SpiderKit.rigTemplateMappings` carry plugin-contributed templates and
  writ-type mappings.
- **Engine designs via kit contributions: yes** — `kit.engines: Record<id,
  EngineDesign>` is the standard shape; the Fabricator scans every kit's
  `engines` field and registers it under the contributing plugin's id.
- **Plugin CLI tools: yes** — tools registered via `supportKit.tools`
  auto-discover into `nsg <tool-name>` (with auto-grouping when names
  share a hyphen prefix). `nsg lab trial post` would come from a tool
  named `lab-trial-post`.
- **gh org permissions: yellow.** Org membership active; token scope is
  `repo`. Repo-create probably works (member-permitted in most org
  policies); repo-delete may not — admin perms needed depending on org
  policy. Need to verify by attempting once before relying on it.

The single piece the apparatus design got wrong is `nsg codex add` —
**it does NOT take a base SHA.** It only takes `(name, remoteUrl)`. The
SHA pinning happens earlier in the flow: the run-init script clones
upstream at the base SHA, pushes that as a fresh GH repo, then `nsg codex
add <new-repo>` clones the fresh repo at HEAD (which IS the base SHA by
construction). The original spec already describes this flow correctly;
the misread was about which step does the pinning.

No design adjustments required from the recon.

## Detailed findings

### 1. `nsg init`

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/commands/init.ts`

Surface: `nsg init <path> [--name <name>] [--model <model>]`

- Creates `<path>/`, `<path>/.nexus/`, `<path>/guild.json`,
  `<path>/package.json`, `<path>/.gitignore`.
- Refuses if `<path>` exists and is non-empty.
- Does NOT git init.
- Does NOT install plugins (separate step).
- Default model is `sonnet`.
- When framework is published (`VERSION !== '0.0.0'`), pins
  `@shardworks/nexus` in deps and runs `npm install`. In dev (link
  mode, VERSION = '0.0.0'), no install.

Gap vs. apparatus design: none. Spec already calls `nsg init <guild-dir>`
and follows up with separate `plugin install` / `codex add` steps.

### 2. `nsg plugin install`

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/commands/plugin.ts`

Surface: `nsg plugin install <source> [--type registry|link]`

Handler logic:
- If source is a path that exists and is a directory → link mode
  (`npm install --save file:<dir>` or `pnpm add link:<dir>`).
- Otherwise → registry mode (`npm install --save <source>`).

The `<source>` string is passed unchanged to npm. So:
- `@shardworks/foo@1.2.3` — semver works
- `@shardworks/foo@latest` — dist-tag works
- `git+https://github.com/foo/bar#<sha>` — SHA pin works (npm-native)
- `github:foo/bar#<sha>` — short-form git ref works
- `./local/path` — local link works

Plugin-id derivation: the framework parses the package name out of the
source string; for git URLs it falls back to reading the most recently
added entry in the guild's `package.json` after install (relies on
key insertion order — explicitly noted in code as a known compromise).

Gap vs. apparatus design: none. The handoff prompt's Q "does install
accept git SHAs" is yes, via standard npm git+URL syntax.

### 3. `nsg codex add`

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/codexes/src/tools/codex-add.ts`

Surface: `nsg codex add --name <name> --remote-url <url>` (the auto-
grouping makes positional flags work too).

Handler logic: clones a bare copy to `.nexus/codexes/<name>.git`, registers
`{ remoteUrl }` under `codexes.registered[name]` in guild.json. **No SHA
parameter.** The clone is bare-default (HEAD is whatever the remote points
at).

Gap vs. apparatus design: the spec implied `nsg codex add <repo-url> --sha
<codex-base-sha>`; that flag does not exist. The fix is *order*, not
behavior: clone upstream at base SHA → push to fresh repo → `nsg codex add`
the fresh repo (which is at base SHA by construction). The spec text
"`gh repo create ... --source=<temp-dir> --push`" already describes this
correctly; only the example invocation `nsg codex add <repo-url> --sha
<codex-base-sha>` was misleading.

### 4. Engine designs (the load-bearing question)

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/fabricator/src/fabricator.ts`

```ts
export interface EngineDesign {
  id: string;
  run(givens, context): Promise<EngineRunResult>;
  collect?(sessionId, givens, context): Promise<unknown>;
  retry?: EngineRetryConfig;
}

export type EngineRunResult =
  | { status: 'completed'; yields: unknown }
  | { status: 'launched'; sessionId: string }
  | { status: 'blocked'; blockType: string; condition: unknown; message?: string };
```

The `'completed'` branch is exactly what shell/script-style engines need.
No anima, no session, no Loom involvement — the engine does its work
synchronously in `run()` and returns yields directly. The Spider treats
the result as `engine-completed` and moves on.

Concrete precedent: `spider.seal` (in
`/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/spider/src/engines/seal.ts`) calls
`scriptorium.seal()` synchronously and returns
`{ status: 'completed', yields }`. Multiple Astrolabe engines do the
same (`plan-init`, `plan-finalize`, `inventory-check`, etc.).

Implication for the lab: every fixture, probe, and scenario engine I'll
write is a sync function that does shell/git/network/fs work and returns
yields. No new framework primitive needed.

Engines are registered via the plugin's `supportKit.engines: Record<id,
EngineDesign>`. The Fabricator scans every kit/supportKit's `engines`
field at startup. Two kits contributing the same id throws.

### 5. Writ-type registration

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts`

`ClerkApi.registerWritType(config: WritTypeConfig)` is called from the
plugin's `apparatus.start()`. The registration window closes at
`phase:started`; calling after that throws.

Astrolabe registers `step` and `observation-set` this way. The Laboratory
will register `trial`. The `WritTypeConfig` shape lives in
`writ-type-config.ts` (state machine, child behavior, etc.).

### 6. Rig templates and mappings

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/plugins/spider/src/spider.ts`

```ts
export interface SpiderKit {
  rigTemplates?: Record<string, RigTemplate>;
  rigTemplateMappings?: Record<string, string>;
}
```

Templates are keyed unqualified; the Spider registers them as
`<pluginId>.<key>`. Mappings map writ types to template names.

The Laboratory's `supportKit.rigTemplates` will carry
`'post-and-collect-default'`; mappings will route `trial` to
`'laboratory.post-and-collect-default'` (or whatever the resolved name is —
need to check whether mapping values use qualified or unqualified ids).

### 7. Plugin CLI tools

Source: `/workspace/nexus/packages/framework/cli/src/program.ts`

CLI surface for plugin-contributed tools: registered via the `tools`
apparatus (kit contribution `tools` field). The CLI auto-discovers and
auto-groups by hyphen prefix — `lab-trial-post`, `lab-trial-show`,
`lab-trial-list` would group under `nsg lab` as `trial-post`, etc. (need
to verify the exact grouping behavior — there's a `findGroupPrefixes`
function I didn't read yet).

### 8. gh permissions

Verified directly:
- Org membership in `shardworks`: active (role: member, direct).
- Token scopes: `gist`, `read:org`, `repo`. The `repo` scope is
  sufficient for `gh repo create` and `gh repo delete` IF the org policy
  permits.
- Permissions on `shardworks/nexus-mk2`: `{maintain: true, push: true,
  triage: true, pull: true, admin: false}`. Admin would simplify
  arbitrary repo mgmt; without it we depend on org-level repo policy.

What I haven't verified:
- Whether org members can create new repos in `shardworks` org. (Would
  need an org settings read or a test create.)
- Whether org members can delete repos they created. Default GitHub
  behavior is yes (creator can always delete), but it's worth confirming
  with a test once.

Recommendation: keep this as a yellow flag in the apparatus design.
Test it on the smoke run by attempting a real codex create + delete; if
it fails, surface to Sean and either bump token scopes / org perms or
adjust the apparatus to use a different repo namespace.

## What this changes about the apparatus design

Nothing fundamental. The design is implementable as written. Two minor
adjustments:

1. **`nsg codex add` invocation:** drop the imagined `--sha` flag. The
   codex-add step pins via the repo's HEAD (which is the base SHA after
   the gh repo create + push). The original spec text already describes
   this correctly; only the literal example invocation was wrong.

2. **Codex-fixture engine flow:** Make explicit that the engine must do
   the clone+push+create work BEFORE calling `nsg codex add`. Three
   substeps inside one engine, not three separate engines.

## Open questions for Sean

These are things the recon didn't decide for me; quick answers help shape
the implementation:

1. **Codex repo namespace.** Spec says "same GitHub org as the upstream
   codex" (`shardworks` for the dev case). Confirm that's the answer for
   v1, not e.g. a personal account or a dedicated `shardworks-experiments`
   org.

2. **Codex repo visibility.** Spec says `--private`. Confirm.

3. **Codex naming format.** Spec says
   `experiment-<X-num>-<slug>-run-<NNN>-<variant>`. With trial as the
   primary writ type now (no separate experiment), names will be
   `trial-<trialId-or-slug>` or similar. Need to pick the actual format
   — and decide whether the "experiment" concept survives in the slug
   even though it doesn't have a writ type yet.

4. **Archive target (the still-open design question).** Three options:
   lab-host guild's books, sanctum mirror, or hybrid. Not blocked by
   recon — needs a separate conversation.

content.text:
Oriented. Here's the lay of the land and what I think we should do first.

## Where we stand

The apparatus skeleton is built — phase orchestrators, manifest CLI, smoke test all green. **Every work engine is a stub.** The implementation handoff lists 10 steps; steps 4–5 (probes + archive) are gated on the archive-design conversation in the companion handoff.

```
Steps 1–3 unblocked:  codex / guild / xguild-scenario engines
Step 4–5 blocked:     probes + archive engine (need archive-target decision)
Steps 6–10 follow:    smoke test, retire infra spec, docs, first real trial
```

## Proposed first move — Step 1: `lab.codex-setup` + `lab.codex-teardown`

The handoff's natural order is 1 → 2 → 3 (each step's yields feed the next). Step 1 is mostly mechanical, with **one yellow flag** that gets answered the first time we run it live:

> The `gh` token has `repo` scope and active membership in `shardworks` but `admin: false` on existing repos. Repo-create + delete in the org is unverified.

The first real verify run is also a permissions probe. If the org rejects, we back off to a different namespace per the handoff guidance.

**The engine pair:**
- `lab.codex-setup` — clone upstream at base SHA → `gh repo create` private repo `trial-<slug>-<id-prefix>` in `shardworks` → push → `nsg codex add` (registers the new repo as a guild codex). Yields: `codexName`, `remoteUrl`, `baseSha`, `headSha`, `ownerRepo`.
- `lab.codex-teardown` — `gh repo delete --yes`, refusing if `archive` engine hasn't run for this rig.

**Implementation rhythm (per the handoff's discipline):**
1. Move stubs out of `stubs.ts` → real files under `engines/` (e.g. `codex-fixture.ts`).
2. Unit tests against mocked shell/scriptorium calls.
3. Single commit covering both engines + tests + stub removal.
4. Live-verify by posting a minimal trial in vibers — restart vibers daemon to load the new engine designs.
5. Conclude `c-moma9y1k` in the same commit.

## A few open clarifications worth getting your call on before I start

1. **Codex repo namespace.** Handoff says `shardworks`. Recon flagged a fallback — if the org rejects, do you want me to try `seatec26` (your account) automatically, or stop and surface? My instinct is **stop and surface** — silent namespace migration would be exactly the kind of environment compensation we don't want.

2. **Source-of-truth for the `gh` cli call.** Should the engine shell out to `gh` directly, or use the codexes plugin's API? The recon noted `nsg codex add` only takes `(name, remoteUrl)` — repo creation is fully outside its surface, so direct `gh` shell-out is the only path. Fine to confirm.

3. **Error handling on partial failure.** If `gh repo create` succeeds but `git push` fails — leave the empty remote and let teardown clean up, or attempt rollback in `setup`? My lean is **fail-loud, leave the orphan; teardown handles cleanup.** Engine retries are then idempotent on the fresh attempt (different trial id → different repo name).

Want me to proceed on Step 1 with those defaults (stop-and-surface on namespace, fail-loud on partial), or walk through any of them first?

content: Implementing real Laboratory engines per the handoff at .scratch/handoff-laboratory-implementation.md, starting with Step 1: lab.codex-setup and lab.codex-teardown. Next action: confirm the three open calls (shardworks namespace fallback, direct gh shell-out, fail-loud on partial setup) so I can start coding. (disable recaps in /config)

Do not fallback.. if there is a permission error, surface it and i will resolve. if it happens at runtime, consider it a runtime failure of the engine. All that said....... do we actually _need_ github remotes for the codexes? couldnt we just use a bare repo locally? is there a way to speficy a folder as a git remote url? (2) determine answers to #1 and reassess. (3) fail-loud, esp for now

content.text:
Excellent question. Yes — and I think this is the right move. Local bare repo works cleanly.

## What I found

`nsg codex add` invokes `git clone --bare <remoteUrl> <path>` with the URL passed unchanged to git. **No URL parsing, no protocol validation.** Git natively accepts local paths and `file://` URLs as remotes. Same goes for push — a bare local repo is a valid push target by design.

The "remoteUrl" name is a misnomer in the codexes plugin — it's just a clone source string. Sean was right to push on this.

## Proposed local-only flow

**Trial codex setup engine:**
1. Clone upstream (whatever the user specifies — could be `shardworks/nexus-mk2` over network, or `/workspace/nexus-mk2` locally) into a temp dir.
2. `git checkout <baseSha>` in the temp dir.
3. `git init --bare <trial-bare-path>` — bare repo at a per-trial path.
4. `git push <trial-bare-path> HEAD:main` from temp to seed it.
5. Remove temp dir.
6. `nsg codex add <codexName> <trial-bare-path>` (lab-host guild side) → registers the bare as a guild codex.

**Trial codex teardown:** `rm -rf <trial-bare-path>` (after archive-safety check).

**Where the bare lives:** I'd put it under the lab-host guild's filesystem boundary, e.g. `<labHostGuild>/.nexus/laboratory/codexes/<codexName>.git`. Reasons:
- Co-located with other lab state, easy to inspect/clean.
- `<labHostGuild>` is discoverable from inside the engine via `guild().rootPath`.
- Teardown of the test guild dir doesn't risk taking the codex with it.

**Manifest field rename:** `upstreamRepo: shardworks/nexus` → `upstreamRepo: <git-clone-source>`. Treat the string as opaque; pass unchanged to `git clone`. Users can supply `owner/name` (will resolve via gh's default), a full git URL, OR a local path. For our P3 use case, `/workspace/nexus-mk2` works directly.

## What we win

- **No `gh` dependency.** No org permissions question. No yellow flag.
- **No network** for the dev case (upstream is `/workspace/nexus-mk2` locally).
- **Faster** — local bare init + push is milliseconds vs. network round-trips.
- **Simpler teardown** — `rm -rf` instead of `gh repo delete`.
- **No throwaway pollution** in `shardworks` org.

## What we lose (and why I think it doesn't matter for v1)

- **GitHub UI** for browsing trial commits. → We have `probe-git-range` capturing the diffs into the archive; UI isn't needed.
- **Multi-machine collaboration** on a trial codex. → Trials are single-host by design (the lab-host guild owns the run).
- **Verification that the apparatus works against a real network remote.** → If/when we ever need that, the engine accepts any clone source — point it at a GitHub URL and you're back to the original flow without code changes.

## Yields shape (revised)

```ts
{
  codexName: string,        // the registered codex name in the lab-host guild
  remoteUrl: string,        // absolute path to the trial bare repo
  baseSha: string,          // echoed for downstream
  headSha: string,          // = baseSha at setup; updated by trial work
  // ownerRepo: REMOVED — no longer GitHub-specific
}
```

## Reassessing #2 (clarification on `gh` shell-out)

Moot — no `gh` involvement. The engine uses git directly (we can just exec `git`, or use the Scriptorium's git helper if it's exported and ergonomic).

## Anything I'm missing?

One edge case worth flagging: if the upstream supplied is a local path that's a working tree (like `/workspace/nexus-mk2`), we should clone via `git clone /path` not by reading files directly — git handles working-tree-clone cleanly. Worth noting in tests but not a design concern.

**Want me to proceed with this shape?** I'll write up the engine, tests, and live-verify in vibers. The change cascades to: types.ts (rename/comment update), manifest.ts (no schema change since `upstreamRepo` is just a string), the YAML manifest example, and the smoke-test manifest.

content.content:
1	# The Laboratory
2	
3	Apparatus for running trial-shaped experiments on guild configurations.
4	
5	## Audiences
6	
7	- **Nexus dev** — cost/quality tuning, prompt evaluation, plugin variant
8	  comparison. Replaces the standalone-bash spec at
9	  `experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md`.
10	- **End users** — evaluate prompts, plugins, and config variants by
11	  authoring trial manifests against a stable apparatus surface.
12	
13	## Architecture (MVP0)
14	
15	- **Writ type:** `trial` — a single execution unit. Lifecycle mirrors
16	  mandate (`new → open → completed | failed | cancelled`, with `stuck`
17	  as a non-terminal off `open`). Trials are leaves in v1; the
18	  higher-level `experiment` grouping is parked for v2.
19	- **Rig template:** `post-and-collect-default` — composes
20	  fixture-setup, scenario, probe, teardown, and archive engines from
21	  the writ's `ext.laboratory.config`. One canonical template; extension
22	  is via plugin contributions, not in-template slots.
23	- **Engines:**
24	  - **Fixtures** — set up and tear down disposable surfaces (codex
25	    repos, test guilds). Form a dep DAG; topo-sorted at template
26	    instantiation.
27	  - **Scenario** — the workload. v1 uses cross-guild commission-post +
28	    wait-for-writ-terminal as the canonical scenario engine pair.
29	  - **Probes** — extract data from one or more fixtures (stacks dump,
30	    git range capture).
31	  - **Archive** — captures probe outputs for the research record.
32	    Storage layout in design at click `c-momaa5o9`.
33	- **Authoring:** YAML manifest via `nsg lab trial post --manifest <file>`.
34	  Manifest shape mirrors `ext.laboratory.config` exactly.
35	
36	## Status
37	
38	Skeleton only. The trial writ type is registered; engine designs, the
39	rig template, and the manifest CLI are added by subsequent
40	implementation children under click `c-moma9llq`.
41	
42	## Background
43	
44	This package previously held a CDC-based observational stub (data
45	mirroring of writs/sessions into the sanctum). That instrument was
46	retired 2026-04-30 — its underlying signals (patron-set spec quality
47	ratings, structured commission review) had been hollowed out by the
48	shift to automated planning and static review pipelines. The package
49	was kept as a no-op so existing `guild.json` registrations stayed
50	loadable; the apparatus reshape reuses the package and the registered
51	plugin id, but the old data-mirroring code and types are gone.
52

file.content:
# The Laboratory

Apparatus for running trial-shaped experiments on guild configurations.

## Audiences

- **Nexus dev** — cost/quality tuning, prompt evaluation, plugin variant
  comparison. Replaces the standalone-bash spec at
  `experiments/infrastructure/setup-and-artifacts.md`.
- **End users** — evaluate prompts, plugins, and config variants by
  authoring trial manifests against a stable apparatus surface.

## Architecture (MVP0)

- **Writ type:** `trial` — a single execution unit. Lifecycle mirrors
  mandate (`new → open → completed | failed | cancelled`, with `stuck`
  as a non-terminal off `open`). Trials are leaves in v1; the
  higher-level `experiment` grouping is parked for v2.
- **Rig template:** `post-and-collect-default` — composes
  fixture-setup, scenario, probe, teardown, and archive engines from
  the writ's `ext.laboratory.config`. One canonical template; extension
  is via plugin contributions, not in-template slots.
- **Engines:**
  - **Fixtures** — set up and tear down disposable surfaces (codex
    repos, test guilds). Form a dep DAG; topo-sorted at template
    instantiation.
  - **Scenario** — the workload. v1 uses cross-guild commission-post +
    wait-for-writ-terminal as the canonical scenario engine pair.
  - **Probes** — extract data from one or more fixtures (stacks dump,
    git range capture).
  - **Archive** — captures probe outputs for the research record.
    Storage layout in design at click `c-momaa5o9`.
- **Authoring:** YAML manifest via `nsg lab trial post --manifest <file>`.
  Manifest shape mirrors `ext.laboratory.config` exactly.

## Status

Skeleton only. The trial writ type is registered; engine designs, the
rig template, and the manifest CLI are added by subsequent
implementation children under click `c-moma9llq`.

## Background

This package previously held a CDC-based observational stub (data
mirroring of writs/sessions into the sanctum). That instrument was
retired 2026-04-30 — its underlying signals (patron-set spec quality
ratings, structured commission review) had been hollowed out by the
shift to automated planning and static review pipelines. The package
was kept as a no-op so existing `guild.json` registrations stayed
loadable; the apparatus reshape reuses the package and the registered
plugin id, but the old data-mirroring code and types are gone.

1	/**
2	 * Guild — the process-level singleton for accessing guild infrastructure.
3	 *
4	 * All plugin code — apparatus start(), tool handlers, engine handlers,
5	 * relay handlers, CDC handlers — imports `guild()` to access apparatus APIs,
6	 * plugin config, the guild root path, and the loaded plugin graph.
7	 *
8	 * Arbor creates the Guild instance before starting apparatus and registers
9	 * it via `setGuild()`. The instance is backed by live data structures
10	 * (e.g. the provides Map) that are populated progressively as apparatus start.
11	 *
12	 * The `Guild` interface is the contract plugin code sees — it deliberately
13	 * does not expose `shutdown()`. Lifecycle teardown is the responsibility
14	 * of the bootstrapping process (the CLI, a daemon entry point, a one-shot
15	 * helper) that called `createGuild()`. That caller receives a
16	 * `StartedGuild` (a `Guild` plus a `shutdown()` method) and is expected
17	 * to invoke it before exit. See `StartedGuild` and `clearGuild()` below.
18	 *
19	 * See: docs/architecture/plugins.md
20	 */
21	
22	import type { GuildConfig } from './guild-config.ts';
23	import type { LoadedKit, LoadedApparatus, FailedPlugin } from './plugin.ts';
24	
25	// ── Interface ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
26	
27	/**
28	 * Runtime access to guild infrastructure.
29	 *
30	 * Available after Arbor creates the instance (before apparatus start).
31	 * One instance per process. Plugin code only ever sees this narrow
32	 * interface — `shutdown()` is intentionally not part of it. The
33	 * bootstrap caller of `createGuild()` receives the richer
34	 * `StartedGuild` and owns the shutdown lifecycle.
35	 */
36	export interface Guild {
37	  /** Absolute path to the guild root (contains guild.json). */
38	  readonly home: string
39	
40	  /**
41	   * Retrieve a started apparatus's provides object by plugin id.
42	   *
43	   * Throws if the apparatus is not installed or has no `provides`.
44	   * During startup, only apparatus that have already started are visible
45	   * (dependency ordering guarantees declared deps are started first).
46	   */
47	  apparatus<T>(name: string): T
48	
49	  /**
50	   * Retrieve a started apparatus's provides object by plugin id, or
51	   * `null` if the apparatus is not installed (or has no `provides`).
52	   *
53	   * The optional counterpart to {@link Guild.apparatus} — same arity,
54	   * same generic, same caller ergonomics. Reach for this when the
55	   * apparatus is in `recommends`, not `requires`, so the caller can
56	   * branch on its presence rather than catching a thrown error.
57	   *
58	   *   const clockworks = guild().tryApparatus<ClockworksApi>('clockworks');
59	   *   if (!clockworks) return; // optional dependency, soft no-op
60	   *
61	   * Use {@link Guild.apparatus} when the apparatus is a hard requirement
62	   * (declared in `requires`) — its throw-on-missing behaviour fails
63	   * loudly at the call site rather than silently no-op'ing past a
64	   * misconfiguration.
65	   */
66	  tryApparatus<T>(name: string): T | null
67	
68	  /**
69	   * Read a plugin's configuration section from guild.json.
70	   *
71	   * Returns `guild.json[pluginId]` cast to `T`. Returns `{}` if no
72	   * section exists. The generic parameter is a cast — the framework
73	   * does not validate config shape.
74	   */
75	  config<T = Record<string, unknown>>(pluginId: string): T
76	
77	  /**
78	   * Write a plugin's configuration section to guild.json.
79	   *
80	   * Updates `guild.json[pluginId]` with `value` and writes the file
81	   * to disk. Also updates the in-memory config so subsequent reads
82	   * reflect the change.
83	   *
84	   * For framework-level keys (name, nexus, plugins, settings), use
85	   * the standalone `writeGuildConfig()` function instead.
86	   */
87	  writeConfig<T = Record<string, unknown>>(pluginId: string, value: T): void
88	
89	  /**
90	   * Read the full parsed guild.json.
91	   *
92	   * Escape hatch for framework-level fields (name, nexus, plugins,
93	   * settings) that don't belong to any specific plugin.
94	   */
95	  guildConfig(): GuildConfig
96	
97	  /** Snapshot of all loaded standalone kit plugins. Does not include apparatus supportKits. */
98	  kits(): LoadedKit[]
99	
100	  /** Snapshot of all started apparatuses. */
101	  apparatuses(): LoadedApparatus[]
102	
103	  /** Snapshot of plugins that failed to load, validate, or start. */
104	  failedPlugins(): FailedPlugin[]
105	
106	  /** Advisory warnings collected during guild startup (missing recommends, unconsumed contributions). */
107	  startupWarnings(): string[]
108	}
109	
110	/**
111	 * Extension of {@link Guild} returned by `createGuild()` — adds the
112	 * `shutdown()` method that drives reverse-topo apparatus teardown.
113	 *
114	 * The `Guild` interface is what plugin code sees through the
115	 * process-level singleton; `shutdown()` is deliberately not exposed
116	 * there because plugin code has no legitimate reason to tear down the
117	 * guild it is running inside. The bootstrap caller of `createGuild()`
118	 * — a CLI command, a daemon entry point, or a one-shot helper —
119	 * receives this richer type and is responsible for invoking
120	 * `shutdown()` on the way out.
121	 *
122	 * `shutdown()` invokes every started apparatus's optional `stop()` in
123	 * reverse topological order, fires the `guild:shutdown` lifecycle
124	 * event before any `stop()` runs, collects per-apparatus errors and
125	 * surfaces them as a single aggregate (continuing iteration even when
126	 * one throws), is idempotent under repeated calls, and clears the
127	 * `guild()` singleton as its last act so subsequent `guild()` calls
128	 * fail loudly with the existing "Guild not initialized" error rather
129	 * than handing out stale references to apparatus whose handles are
130	 * already gone.
131	 */
132	export interface StartedGuild extends Guild {
133	  /**
134	   * Tear the guild down: fire `guild:shutdown`, call `stop()` on every
135	   * started apparatus in reverse topological order, then clear the
136	   * `guild()` singleton.
137	   *
138	   * Idempotent — second and subsequent calls return immediately.
139	   *
140	   * If one or more `stop()` invocations throw, every remaining
141	   * apparatus is still attempted; once iteration completes, an
142	   * aggregate `Error` is thrown summarising each failure. The
143	   * singleton is cleared regardless of whether any `stop()` threw.
144	   */
145	  shutdown(): Promise<void>
146	}
147	
148	// ── Singleton ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
149	
150	let _guild: Guild | null = null;
151	
152	/**
153	 * Get the active guild instance.
154	 *
155	 * Throws with a clear message if called before Arbor has initialized
156	 * the guild (e.g. at module import time, before startup begins).
157	 */
158	export function guild(): Guild {
159	  if (!_guild) {
160	    throw new Error(
161	      'Guild not initialized — guild() called before Arbor startup. ' +
162	      'Ensure guild() is called inside a handler or start(), not at module scope.',
163	    );
164	  }
165	  return _guild;
166	}
167	
168	/**
169	 * Set the guild instance. Called by Arbor before starting apparatus.
170	 *
171	 * Not for plugin use — this is framework infrastructure.
172	 */
173	export function setGuild(g: Guild): void {
174	  _guild = g;
175	}
176	
177	/**
178	 * Clear the guild instance.
179	 *
180	 * Called as the last act of `StartedGuild.shutdown()` after every
181	 * apparatus's optional `stop()` has run, so subsequent `guild()` calls
182	 * fail loudly with the "Guild not initialized" error rather than
183	 * handing out stale references to apparatus whose handles are gone.
184	 * Tests call it directly to reset between cases.
185	 *
186	 * Not for plugin use — this is framework infrastructure. Plugin code
187	 * should never need to tear the guild down.
188	 */
189	export function clearGuild(): void {
190	  _guild = null;
191	}

/**
 * Guild — the process-level singleton for accessing guild infrastructure.
 *
 * All plugin code — apparatus start(), tool handlers, engine handlers,
 * relay handlers, CDC handlers — imports `guild()` to access apparatus APIs,
 * plugin config, the guild root path, and the loaded plugin graph.
 *
 * Arbor creates the Guild instance before starting apparatus and registers
 * it via `setGuild()`. The instance is backed by live data structures
 * (e.g. the provides Map) that are populated progressively as apparatus start.
 *
 * The `Guild` interface is the contract plugin code sees — it deliberately
 * does not expose `shutdown()`. Lifecycle teardown is the responsibility
 * of the bootstrapping process (the CLI, a daemon entry point, a one-shot
 * helper) that called `createGuild()`. That caller receives a
 * `StartedGuild` (a `Guild` plus a `shutdown()` method) and is expected
 * to invoke it before exit. See `StartedGuild` and `clearGuild()` below.
 *
 * See: docs/architecture/plugins.md
 */

import type { GuildConfig } from './guild-config.ts';
import type { LoadedKit, LoadedApparatus, FailedPlugin } from './plugin.ts';

// ── Interface ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

/**
 * Runtime access to guild infrastructure.
 *
 * Available after Arbor creates the instance (before apparatus start).
 * One instance per process. Plugin code only ever sees this narrow
 * interface — `shutdown()` is intentionally not part of it. The
 * bootstrap caller of `createGuild()` receives the richer
 * `StartedGuild` and owns the shutdown lifecycle.
 */
export interface Guild {
  /** Absolute path to the guild root (contains guild.json). */
  readonly home: string

  /**
   * Retrieve a started apparatus's provides object by plugin id.
   *
   * Throws if the apparatus is not installed or has no `provides`.
   * During startup, only apparatus that have already started are visible
   * (dependency ordering guarantees declared deps are started first).
   */
  apparatus<T>(name: string): T

  /**
   * Retrieve a started apparatus's provides object by plugin id, or
   * `null` if the apparatus is not installed (or has no `provides`).
   *
   * The optional counterpart to {@link Guild.apparatus} — same arity,
   * same generic, same caller ergonomics. Reach for this when the
   * apparatus is in `recommends`, not `requires`, so the caller can
   * branch on its presence rather than catching a thrown error.
   *
   *   const clockworks = guild().tryApparatus<ClockworksApi>('clockworks');
   *   if (!clockworks) return; // optional dependency, soft no-op
   *
   * Use {@link Guild.apparatus} when the apparatus is a hard requirement
   * (declared in `requires`) — its throw-on-missing behaviour fails
   * loudly at the call site rather than silently no-op'ing past a
   * misconfiguration.
   */
  tryApparatus<T>(name: string): T | null

  /**
   * Read a plugin's configuration section from guild.json.
   *
   * Returns `guild.json[pluginId]` cast to `T`. Returns `{}` if no
   * section exists. The generic parameter is a cast — the framework
   * does not validate config shape.
   */
  config<T = Record<string, unknown>>(pluginId: string): T

  /**
   * Write a plugin's configuration section to guild.json.
   *
   * Updates `guild.json[pluginId]` with `value` and writes the file
   * to disk. Also updates the in-memory config so subsequent reads
   * reflect the change.
   *
   * For framework-level keys (name, nexus, plugins, settings), use
   * the standalone `writeGuildConfig()` function instead.
   */
  writeConfig<T = Record<string, unknown>>(pluginId: string, value: T): void

  /**
   * Read the full parsed guild.json.
   *
   * Escape hatch for framework-level fields (name, nexus, plugins,
   * settings) that don't belong to any specific plugin.
   */
  guildConfig(): GuildConfig

  /** Snapshot of all loaded standalone kit plugins. Does not include apparatus supportKits. */
  kits(): LoadedKit[]

  /** Snapshot of all started apparatuses. */
  apparatuses(): LoadedApparatus[]

  /** Snapshot of plugins that failed to load, validate, or start. */
  failedPlugins(): FailedPlugin[]

  /** Advisory warnings collected during guild startup (missing recommends, unconsumed contributions). */
  startupWarnings(): string[]
}

/**
 * Extension of {@link Guild} returned by `createGuild()` — adds the
 * `shutdown()` method that drives reverse-topo apparatus teardown.
 *
 * The `Guild` interface is what plugin code sees through the
 * process-level singleton; `shutdown()` is deliberately not exposed
 * there because plugin code has no legitimate reason to tear down the
 * guild it is running inside. The bootstrap caller of `createGuild()`
 * — a CLI command, a daemon entry point, or a one-shot helper —
 * receives this richer type and is responsible for invoking
 * `shutdown()` on the way out.
 *
 * `shutdown()` invokes every started apparatus's optional `stop()` in
 * reverse topological order, fires the `guild:shutdown` lifecycle
 * event before any `stop()` runs, collects per-apparatus errors and
 * surfaces them as a single aggregate (continuing iteration even when
 * one throws), is idempotent under repeated calls, and clears the
 * `guild()` singleton as its last act so subsequent `guild()` calls
 * fail loudly with the existing "Guild not initialized" error rather
 * than handing out stale references to apparatus whose handles are
 * already gone.
 */
export interface StartedGuild extends Guild {
  /**
   * Tear the guild down: fire `guild:shutdown`, call `stop()` on every
   * started apparatus in reverse topological order, then clear the
   * `guild()` singleton.
   *
   * Idempotent — second and subsequent calls return immediately.
   *
   * If one or more `stop()` invocations throw, every remaining
   * apparatus is still attempted; once iteration completes, an
   * aggregate `Error` is thrown summarising each failure. The
   * singleton is cleared regardless of whether any `stop()` threw.
   */
  shutdown(): Promise<void>
}

// ── Singleton ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

let _guild: Guild | null = null;

/**
 * Get the active guild instance.
 *
 * Throws with a clear message if called before Arbor has initialized
 * the guild (e.g. at module import time, before startup begins).
 */
export function guild(): Guild {
  if (!_guild) {
    throw new Error(
      'Guild not initialized — guild() called before Arbor startup. ' +
      'Ensure guild() is called inside a handler or start(), not at module scope.',
    );
  }
  return _guild;
}

/**
 * Set the guild instance. Called by Arbor before starting apparatus.
 *
 * Not for plugin use — this is framework infrastructure.
 */
export function setGuild(g: Guild): void {
  _guild = g;
}

/**
 * Clear the guild instance.
 *
 * Called as the last act of `StartedGuild.shutdown()` after every
 * apparatus's optional `stop()` has run, so subsequent `guild()` calls
 * fail loudly with the "Guild not initialized" error rather than
 * handing out stale references to apparatus whose handles are gone.
 * Tests call it directly to reset between cases.
 *
 * Not for plugin use — this is framework infrastructure. Plugin code
 * should never need to tear the guild down.
 */
export function clearGuild(): void {
  _guild = null;
}

/**
 * Engine bag — every engine the Laboratory contributes via supportKit.
 *
 * Keys are the engine design ids the Spider's template references.
 * The Fabricator scans this object at startup and registers each
 * design under the laboratory plugin id.
 *
 * The five phase orchestrators (`lab.{setup,scenario,probes,archive,
 * teardown}-phase`) form the rig template's static backbone; they
 * read `writ.ext.laboratory.config` at run time and emit per-phase
 * grafts. The other engines (codex/guild fixtures, scenario,
 * probes, archive) are the work engines those grafts reference.
 *
 * Stubs land their real implementations under their respective
 * implementation children.
 */

import type { EngineDesign } from '@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus';
import { phaseEngines } from './phases.ts';
import {
  archiveStub,
  codexSetupStub,
  codexTeardownStub,
  commissionPostXguildStub,
  guildSetupStub,
  guildTeardownStub,
  probeGitRangeStub,
  probeStacksDumpStub,
  waitForWritTerminalXguildStub,
} from './stubs.ts';

export const engines: Record<string, EngineDesign> = {
  // Phase orchestrators (template backbone)
  ...phaseEngines,

  // Work engines (graft targets)
  'lab.codex-setup': codexSetupStub,
  'lab.codex-teardown': codexTeardownStub,
  'lab.guild-setup': guildSetupStub,
  'lab.guild-teardown': guildTeardownStub,
  'lab.commission-post-xguild': commissionPostXguildStub,
  'lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild': waitForWritTerminalXguildStub,
  'lab.probe-stacks-dump': probeStacksDumpStub,
  'lab.probe-git-range': probeGitRangeStub,
  'lab.archive': archiveStub,
};

content.content:
1	/**
2	 * Skeleton (stub) engine implementations — for engines that have not yet
3	 * been replaced by real code. Stubs log, yield a placeholder payload,
4	 * and return `{ status: 'completed' }` so the rig template's wiring
5	 * stays exercised end-to-end while implementation lands incrementally.
6	 *
7	 * Remaining stubs and the click that lands their real implementation:
8	 *
9	 *   - lab.guild-setup / lab.guild-teardown          — c-momaa03d
10	 *   - lab.commission-post-xguild
11	 *     lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild             — c-momaa1vt
12	 *   - lab.probe-stacks-dump
13	 *     lab.probe-git-range                            — c-momaa3w7
14	 *   - lab.archive                                    — c-momaa5o9
15	 *
16	 * When swapping a stub for its real implementation, move it to its own
17	 * file under `engines/`; `stubs.ts` shrinks toward empty.
18	 *
19	 * Already replaced:
20	 *   - lab.codex-setup / lab.codex-teardown          (c-moma9y1k)
21	 *     → see `engines/codex-fixture.ts`.
22	 */
23	
24	import type { EngineDesign, EngineRunResult } from '@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus';
25	
26	/** Build a stub engine: logs, yields a placeholder, returns completed. */
27	function stubEngine(designId: string, summary: string): EngineDesign {
28	  return {
29	    id: designId,
30	    async run(givens, context): Promise<EngineRunResult> {
31	      // eslint-disable-next-line no-console
32	      console.log(
33	        `[${designId}] STUB invoked (rig=${context.rigId} engine=${context.engineId}); ` +
34	          `givens keys=[${Object.keys(givens).join(', ')}]; summary="${summary}"`,
35	      );
36	      return {
37	        status: 'completed',
38	        yields: {
39	          stub: true,
40	          designId,
41	          summary,
42	          // Echo a minimal subset of givens so downstream stubs can
43	          // resolve `${yields.<id>.<key>}` templates without crashing.
44	          givenKeys: Object.keys(givens),
45	        },
46	      };
47	    },
48	  };
49	}
50	
51	// ── Fixture engines ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
52	
53	// lab.codex-setup / lab.codex-teardown moved to codex-fixture.ts.
54	
55	export const guildSetupStub = stubEngine(
56	  'lab.guild-setup',
57	  'nsg init + per-pin nsg plugin install + nsg codex add + deep-merge guild.json + copy files',
58	);
59	
60	export const guildTeardownStub = stubEngine(
61	  'lab.guild-teardown',
62	  'rm -rf the test guild dir created by lab.guild-setup',
63	);
64	
65	// ── Scenario engines ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
66	
67	export const commissionPostXguildStub = stubEngine(
68	  'lab.commission-post-xguild',
69	  'shell out to nsg commission-post --guild-root <test-guild> --brief <path>',
70	);
71	
72	export const waitForWritTerminalXguildStub = stubEngine(
73	  'lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild',
74	  'poll writ status across guilds via nsg --guild-root <other> writ show',
75	);
76	
77	// ── Probe engines ────────────────────────────────────────────────────
78	
79	export const probeStacksDumpStub = stubEngine(
80	  'lab.probe-stacks-dump',
81	  'export every book in test guild as JSON-per-table with carve-out for >50KB rows',
82	);
83	
84	export const probeGitRangeStub = stubEngine(
85	  'lab.probe-git-range',
86	  'capture per-commit diffs + commits-manifest.yaml between base and head SHAs',
87	);
88	
89	// ── Archive engine ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
90	
91	export const archiveStub = stubEngine(
92	  'lab.archive',
93	  'archive probe outputs to the configured target (open: lab books vs sanctum vs hybrid; c-momaa5o9)',
94	);

content.text:
## Status — codex fixture engines implemented

**Code:**
- `packages/laboratory/src/engines/codex-fixture.ts` — both engines (real implementations).
- `packages/laboratory/src/engines/codex-fixture.test.ts` — 18 tests.
- Stubs removed for `lab.codex-setup` / `lab.codex-teardown`; engine bag rewired.
- Manifest doc-comment updated to show local-path upstream.

**Quality gates:**
- `npm run typecheck` — clean.
- `npm run build` — clean.
- `npm test` — 71 pass / 0 fail (18 new + 53 existing).

**Test coverage shape:** real git operations against an on-disk upstream, real bare-repo creation. Scriptorium API is stubbed (engine-vs-Scriptorium contract is narrow — list/add/remove). Covers: happy path (bare HEAD = baseSha, yields shape, scriptorium.add called), refusals (existing bare path, name collision), rollback (scriptorium.add fails, bad SHA), givens validation, teardown safety check, teardown tolerance for partial-setup orphans.

**Two implementation notes worth flagging:**

1. **`codexName` is a required given** — no defaulting from fixture id. Defaulting would need phases.ts to inject the fixture id into givens, which felt out of scope for this commit. Manifest authors specify it explicitly. We can revisit if it becomes annoying.

2. **Archive-safety check is structural** — teardown refuses if `context.upstream.archive` is undefined (which would only happen if the rig was misconfigured). Once the archive engine is real (`c-momaa5o9`), the check tightens to `archived === true`. Comment in code points at the click.

## Live verify — need your call

Vibers daemon is **not running**. Per the handoff, after a plugin code change the daemon needs a restart to pick up new engine registrations. Two options:

1. **Skip live verify, commit now.** Tests cover the engine in isolation; the smoke-test pattern (codified in step 7 of the handoff) will exercise the integrated path later. Lower confidence in plugin-load + Scriptorium-real interaction, but those paths are well-trodden.

2. **Start vibers daemon, post a real verify trial, then commit.** Higher confidence, but starting the daemon has guild-wide side effects (any pending rigs would resume crawling, standing-orders fire, etc.).

Either way, the work is committable now. Which do you want?

content: Implementing the laboratory's codex-setup/teardown engines as local-bare-repo fixtures (no GitHub). Code, tests (71 pass), and build are clean; awaiting your call on whether to start vibers daemon for live verification before committing. (disable recaps in /config)

content.text:
Good questions. Let me think through the codex-name auto-gen and then make the changes.

**On `codexName` visibility:** It's mostly internal — used as:
- Directory name on disk (`<labHost>/.nexus/codexes/<name>.git`).
- Registry key in `guild.json`.
- Visible if a human runs `nsg codex list` on the lab-host.

So it's not user-displayed prominently, but a debugger looking at the lab-host guild will see it. Ideally readable enough to map back to a trial.

**My plan for auto-gen:**
- Default = `<slug>-<writId-tail>` where `writId-tail` is 8 chars from the writ id's hash portion.
- Manifest authors can override with explicit `codexName` if they want.
- Plumbing: phases.ts injects `_trial: { slug, writId }` into every fixture's givens at graft time. That makes the trial context available to all fixture engines (codex, guild, future ones) — generic improvement, not codex-specific.
- Underscore prefix marks it as framework-injected.

Let me make the changes.

Body:
  First end-to-end exercise of the post-and-collect-default rig.
  All grafted engines are stubs that yield placeholder data; the

End-to-end success against the loaded daemon code: 9 engines all completed in single attempts, 8.2s wall-clock, real bare repo created at the path I expected, real Scriptorium add+remove, real git operations.

The auto-default + `_trial` injection paths have unit-test coverage but weren't live-tested (those changes were made after your daemon restart). I'll commit on the strength of the unit tests + verified baseline. We can re-verify auto-default with a fresh daemon any time.

content.content:
commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 88f89549-681a-4adf-9989-d1d76a2fdd85
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Refactor laboratory rig template from one-engine flat orchestrator to staged phase orchestrators. After Sean's question about whether the rig template should set the overall structure (vs. one god engine grafting everything), worked through the trade-offs — staged wins on template-as-flow-doc, per-phase failure visibility (oculus shows lab.probes-phase failed instead of opaque orchestrator failure), per-phase logic cohesion (DAG sort vs parallel scatter vs reverse-sequential chain naturally split), and sets up an extension point for future phases (e.g., warmup before scenario). Cost was modest: existing buildGraft was already 5 phase blocks separated by comments. Split into 5 per-phase helpers (buildSetupGraft, buildScenarioGraft, buildProbesGraft, buildArchiveGraft, buildTeardownGraft) plus 5 phase orchestrator engines (lab.setup-phase, lab.scenario-phase, lab.probes-phase, lab.archive-phase, lab.teardown-phase). Template grew from 1 engine to 5 enumerating the flow. graftTail set only on lab.teardown-phase (the rig's true end for completion gating); intermediate phases let the next phase fire immediately, since real work-engines wait via explicit cross-phase upstream refs (scenario.upstream → all fixture-setups; probes.upstream → scenario; archive.upstream → all probes; teardowns chain back from archive). Cross-phase data flow stays at the work-engine layer — phase orchestrators don't relay yields. Sean confirmed: keep categorical config shape; reading-A (new probe engines accommodated automatically via engineId-on-decl) is what mechanical linkage already gives us; reading-B/C (new phases via plugin contribution + engine self-declared phase metadata) is later work. 29 tests now (up from 21), 7 suites — covers each per-phase graft helper plus an end-to-end composition test that asserts the staged phases yield the same flat work-engine list the prior flat orchestrator did. Build clean, typecheck clean."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 88f89549-681a-4adf-9989-d1d76a2fdd85
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Manifest CLI landed (c-moma9ty6) — apparatus is now operable end-to-end via 'nsg lab-trial-post <manifest>'. src/manifest.ts: zod schema mirroring LaboratoryTrialConfig with optional title/description/parentId/codex; parser walks YAML parse → schema validation → fixture DAG cross-validation (reuses topoSortFixtures from phases.ts) → probe id uniqueness; manifestToWritShape splits the parsed manifest into clerk.post inputs + the ext.laboratory.config payload that lands on the writ. src/tools/trial-post.ts: wraps the parser, calls clerk.post(type='trial') with codex auto-resolution mirroring commission-post (inherit from parentId; single-codex default; fail-loud on multiple/none), then clerk.setWritExt('laboratory', {config}), then clerk.transition('new'→'open') unless --draft. lab-trial-post wired into supportKit.tools (untyped array per clerk's precedent — ToolDefinition is generic over the schema shape). 26 manifest tests added bringing total to 55 (12 suites): YAML parse errors with helpful messages (no zod tracebacks for syntactic problems), schema rejections (slug shape including digit-start and length cap, missing required fields, malformed ids, mutability v2-reserved enum), defaults (empty fixtures/probes/givens), DAG cross-validation, probe id uniqueness, manifestToWritShape pure-data transform. CLI surface verified live via 'nsg --guild-root /workspace/vibers lab-trial-post --help' — manifest is auto-promoted to positional argument (single-required-string rule); --draft is a flag. Form is 'nsg lab-trial-post' not 'nsg lab trial-post' because the framework's prefix grouping requires ≥2 sibling tools (lone lab-trial-post stays flat); will auto-group once a second lab-prefixed tool lands. Live trial posting deferred until vibers daemon restart so the running spider has the new engine + rig template registrations. Slug constraints: lowercase kebab-case starting with letter, alphanumeric+hyphen, ≤40 chars — keeps 'trial-<slug>-<trialId-prefix>' codex/GH-repo names under 63-char DNS limit with margin. Yaml lib chosen ('yaml' not 'js-yaml'): matches framework precedent (cartograph + spider use 'yaml'). Zod pinned to 4.3.6 to align with clerk's version."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: 88f89549-681a-4adf-9989-d1d76a2fdd85
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Live end-to-end smoke verification of the laboratory apparatus against vibers (after Sean restarted the daemon to pick up the new plugin code). Pre-checks: trial writ type registered (writ types now mandate, step, observation-set, trial); single 'nexus' codex auto-resolves; nsg lab-trial-post --help shows correct positional + flag surface. Spider tick picked it up ~5s later; rig rig-momencrs-bfc587fd ran every engine to completion in 22ms (created 04:20:46.313Z, terminalAt 04:20:46.335Z). Engine list (13 total) matches expected staged-phase composition: 5 phase orchestrators (setup-phase, scenario-phase, probes-phase, archive-phase, teardown-phase) + 8 grafted work engines (fixture-codex-setup, fixture-test-guild-setup; scenario; probe-stacks, probe-git; archive; fixture-test-guild-teardown, fixture-codex-teardown). Each ran exactly once, no retries consumed. Trial writ resolved with the last engine's yields (fixture-codex-teardown's stub output) — confirms graftTail propagation works correctly through the staged orchestrators for rig completion gating. The four prior commits' wiring is validated end-to-end: writ-type registration, ext stamping, codex auto-resolution, manifest CLI translation, rig template lookup (trial → laboratory.post-and-collect-default), staged-phase orchestration, dynamic graft emission, fixture topo-sort + reverse-teardown chain, parallel probe scatter, archive consolidation. Apparatus is operable; from here every remaining click is replacing stub engines with real implementations."

1	# Coco Log — Standing Research Instrument
2	#
3	# One entry per unit of work handled directly by Coco (the interactive
4	# collaborator) rather than dispatched as an autonomous commission.
5	# Parallel to the commission log — together they capture the full
6	# picture of how work is routed in the system.
7	#
8	# An entry is created for each commit (or tight cluster of commits
9	# toward a single goal) that Coco produces during a session.
10	#
11	# Fields:
12	#   session        — Coco's Claude session ID (join key to archived transcripts)
13	#   date           — date the work was done (YYYY-MM-DD)
14	#   item           — short description of the work (one line)
15	#   commissionable — whether this work could have been an autonomous commission

- session: cb9f1554-a5f1-4144-88e9-6170b3cc653b
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "First real engine pair lands — lab.codex-setup + lab.codex-teardown (concludes c-moma9y1k). Sean reframed the design before any GitHub work happened: trial codexes don't need network remotes, a local bare repo works as a git remote (git clone --bare accepts plain paths; nsg codex add passes its remoteUrl unchanged to git). Drops the gh dependency entirely — no token scope checks, no org-permission yellow flag, no shardworks pollution from throwaway trial repos, faster setup, simpler teardown. New engines/codex-fixture.ts: setup clones upstreamRepo (any git clone source — owner/name, URL, or absolute path) → checkout baseSha → init bare at <labHost>/.nexus/laboratory/codexes/<name>.git → push HEAD:main → register via Scriptorium.add. Fail-loud, all-or-nothing — any mid-flight error triggers rollback (unregister + rm -rf bare + rm -rf temp). Teardown: archive-presence safety check (refuses if context.upstream.archive is absent — structurally indicates rig misconfiguration; tightens to archived===true once the archive engine is real per c-momaa5o9), then Scriptorium.remove + rm -rf bare. Both engines tolerant of partial-setup orphans (codex-not-registered, bare-not-existing). codexName auto-defaults to <slug>-<writId-tail-8> when not supplied; phases.ts now injects _trial:{slug, writId, fixtureId} into every grafted work engine's givens (general improvement — lights up trial-aware defaulting for guild-setup and future fixture engines too, not just codex). 18 unit tests for codex-fixture (real git ops against on-disk upstream + mocked Scriptorium API; covers happy path, auto-default, refusals, rollback, validation, teardown safety + tolerance), 5 phases tests updated to assert _trial injection. Stub-engine console logging removed (was cluttering daemon logs under concurrent rigs). Live verified end-to-end against vibers: 9-engine rig completed in 8.2s with real bare creation+removal; auto-default path covered by unit tests, didn't live-verify (would need fresh daemon restart since the loaded code predates the auto-default edits). Companion handoff for the design conversation that unblocks probes+archive (c-momaa5o9) sits at .scratch/handoff-laboratory-archive-design.md — separate thread."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

Both engines tolerant of partial-setup orphans (codex-not-registered, bare-not-existing). codexName auto-defaults to <slug>-<writId-tail-8> when not supplied; phases.ts now injects _trial:{slug, writId, fixtureId} into every grafted work engine's givens (general improvement — lights up trial-aware defaulting for guild-setup and future fixture engines too, not just codex). 18 unit tests for codex-fixture (real git ops against on-disk upstream + mocked Scriptorium API; covers happy path, auto-default, refusals, rollback, validation, teardown safety + tolerance), 5 phases tests updated to assert _trial injection. Stub-engine console logging removed (was cluttering daemon logs under concurrent rigs). Live verified end-to-end against vibers: 9-engine rig completed in 8.2s with real bare creation+removal; auto-default path covered by unit tests, didn't live-verify (would need fresh daemon restart since the loaded code predates the auto-default edits). Companion handoff for the design conversation that unblocks probes+archive (c-momaa5o9) sits at .scratch/handoff-laboratory-archive-design.md — separate thread."

laboratory: implement codex fixture engines (local-bare flow)

Replaces the lab.codex-setup / lab.codex-teardown stubs with real
implementations that use a local bare repo as the trial codex's
remote — git clone --bare accepts plain paths, nsg codex add passes
remoteUrl unchanged to git, so no GitHub round-trip is needed for
v1. Drops the gh dependency and the org-permission yellow flag.

Setup: clone upstreamRepo (owner/name, URL, or absolute path) →
checkout baseSha → git init --bare at
<labHost>/.nexus/laboratory/codexes/<name>.git → push HEAD:main →
Scriptorium.add. All-or-nothing: rollback on any mid-flight error.

Teardown: archive-presence safety check (refuses when
context.upstream.archive is absent; structurally guarantees the
rig was wired with archive in the chain — tightens once the
archive engine is real per c-momaa5o9), then Scriptorium.remove +
rm -rf. Tolerant of partial-setup orphans.

codexName auto-defaults to <slug>-<writId-tail-8>. Phases.ts now
injects _trial:{slug, writId, fixtureId} into every grafted
work-engine's givens — general improvement that lights up
trial-aware defaulting for future fixture engines too, not just
codex.

Stub engines no longer log to console (was cluttering daemon
logs under concurrent rigs).

Live verified end-to-end against vibers: 9-engine rig completed
in 8.2s with real bare creation + removal.

Concludes click c-moma9y1k.

Session: cb9f1554-a5f1-4144-88e9-6170b3cc653b
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -10

We can re-verify next time you restart the daemon (would naturally happen for the next engine pair anyway).

## Next up

Step 2 of the handoff: **`lab.guild-setup` + `lab.guild-teardown`** (`c-momaa03d`). This is the gnarliest of the unblocked engines — `nsg init` + plugin-install loop + codex registration + guild.json deep-merge + file copies. Want me to keep going on Step 2, or pause here?

content.text:
OK, design decisions locked. Going to write.

**Design summary** (briefly, for the record before code):
- **Guild path default** = `<labHost>/.nexus/laboratory/guilds/<guildName>/` (lab-host-internal, mirrors codex placement). Override via `givens.guildPath`. Differs from the handoff's `/workspace/experiments/...` — I'll note in commit; can switch later if Sean prefers.
- **`guildName` auto-default** = same `<slug>-<writId-tail>` pattern as codex, from `_trial`.
- **`nsg init`** = shell out (`execFile nsg init <path>`).
- **Plugins** = manifest shape `{name, version}`; engine concatenates to `<name>@<version>` and shells `nsg --guild-root <path> plugin install <spec>`.
- **Codex registration** = duck-type discovery from `context.upstream` (anything with `codexName + remoteUrl` gets registered in the test guild via shell-out).
- **Config merge** = read+deep-merge+write `guild.json`.
- **File copies** = absolute `sourcePath` only for v1 (throw on relative). Manifest-relative resolution = future polish.
- **Rollback** = `rm -rf guildPath` on any setup error.
- **Teardown** = archive-presence check + `rm -rf guildPath`.
Now tests:

/**
 * Engine bag — every engine the Laboratory contributes via supportKit.
 *
 * Keys are the engine design ids the Spider's template references.
 * The Fabricator scans this object at startup and registers each
 * design under the laboratory plugin id.
 *
 * The five phase orchestrators (`lab.{setup,scenario,probes,archive,
 * teardown}-phase`) form the rig template's static backbone; they
 * read `writ.ext.laboratory.config` at run time and emit per-phase
 * grafts. The other engines (codex/guild fixtures, scenario,
 * probes, archive) are the work engines those grafts reference.
 *
 * Stubs land their real implementations under their respective
 * implementation children.
 */

import type { EngineDesign } from '@shardworks/fabricator-apparatus';
import { phaseEngines } from './phases.ts';
import { codexSetupEngine, codexTeardownEngine } from './codex-fixture.ts';
import {
  archiveStub,
  commissionPostXguildStub,
  guildSetupStub,
  guildTeardownStub,
  probeGitRangeStub,
  probeStacksDumpStub,
  waitForWritTerminalXguildStub,
} from './stubs.ts';

export const engines: Record<string, EngineDesign> = {
  // Phase orchestrators (template backbone)
  ...phaseEngines,

  // Work engines (graft targets)
  'lab.codex-setup': codexSetupEngine,
  'lab.codex-teardown': codexTeardownEngine,
  'lab.guild-setup': guildSetupStub,
  'lab.guild-teardown': guildTeardownStub,
  'lab.commission-post-xguild': commissionPostXguildStub,
  'lab.wait-for-writ-terminal-xguild': waitForWritTerminalXguildStub,
  'lab.probe-stacks-dump': probeStacksDumpStub,
  'lab.probe-git-range': probeGitRangeStub,
  'lab.archive': archiveStub,
};

input.old_string:
- session: cb9f1554-a5f1-4144-88e9-6170b3cc653b
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "First real engine pair lands — lab.codex-setup + lab.codex-teardown (concludes c-moma9y1k). Sean reframed the design before any GitHub work happened: trial codexes don't need network remotes, a local bare repo works as a git remote (git clone --bare accepts plain paths; nsg codex add passes its remoteUrl unchanged to git). Drops the gh dependency entirely — no token scope checks, no org-permission yellow flag, no shardworks pollution from throwaway trial repos, faster setup, simpler teardown. New engines/codex-fixture.ts: setup clones upstreamRepo (any git clone source — owner/name, URL, or absolute path) → checkout baseSha → init bare at <labHost>/.nexus/laboratory/codexes/<name>.git → push HEAD:main → register via Scriptorium.add. Fail-loud, all-or-nothing — any mid-flight error triggers rollback (unregister + rm -rf bare + rm -rf temp). Teardown: archive-presence safety check (refuses if context.upstream.archive is absent — structurally indicates rig misconfiguration; tightens to archived===true once the archive engine is real per c-momaa5o9), then Scriptorium.remove + rm -rf bare. Both engines tolerant of partial-setup orphans (codex-not-registered, bare-not-existing). codexName auto-defaults to <slug>-<writId-tail-8> when not supplied; phases.ts now injects _trial:{slug, writId, fixtureId} into every grafted work engine's givens (general improvement — lights up trial-aware defaulting for guild-setup and future fixture engines too, not just codex). 18 unit tests for codex-fixture (real git ops against on-disk upstream + mocked Scriptorium API; covers happy path, auto-default, refusals, rollback, validation, teardown safety + tolerance), 5 phases tests updated to assert _trial injection. Stub-engine console logging removed (was cluttering daemon logs under concurrent rigs). Live verified end-to-end against vibers: 9-engine rig completed in 8.2s with real bare creation+removal; auto-default path covered by unit tests, didn't live-verify (would need fresh daemon restart since the loaded code predates the auto-default edits). Companion handoff for the design conversation that unblocks probes+archive (c-momaa5o9) sits at .scratch/handoff-laboratory-archive-design.md — separate thread."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum

- session: cb9f1554-a5f1-4144-88e9-6170b3cc653b
  date: 2026-05-01
  item: "Second real engine pair lands — lab.guild-setup + lab.guild-teardown (concludes c-momaa03d). engines/guild-fixture.ts: setup runs `nsg init <path>` → per-pin `nsg --guild-root <path> plugin install <name>@<version>` (sequential — npm install in same dir doesn't tolerate concurrency, peer/optional deps order-sensitive) → codex registration via duck-typed discovery from context.upstream (any upstream yield with codexName+remoteUrl gets `nsg codex add` in the test guild — no manifest-level dependsOn declaration of 'this is the codex' needed) → deep-merge givens.config into guild.json → copy givens.files entries from absolute sourcePath to <guildPath>/<entry.guildPath>. Fail-loud all-or-nothing rollback (rm -rf the guild dir) on any mid-flight error. Teardown: archive-presence safety check (mirrors codex-teardown discipline) + rm -rf. Auto-defaulting matches codex pattern: guildName defaults to <slug>-<writId-tail-8> from _trial; guildPath defaults to <labHostGuild>/.nexus/laboratory/guilds/<name>/ — lab-host-internal placement consistent with codex bare repo's home. Departs from the handoff's /workspace/experiments/<slug>-<id-prefix>/ default — preferred lab-host-internal for filesystem-cleanup symmetry; can be overridden via givens.guildPath if sanctum-side visibility wins. v1 limitation: file copies require absolute sourcePath (throws on relative); manifest-relative resolution is future polish that would require manifest CLI to walk fixture givens at post time and rewrite paths. 27 unit tests covering deepMerge (5 cases: flat, nested, array replacement, type-mismatch, no-mutation), discoverCodexes (6 cases: empty, single, multiple, mixed-shape upstream, type-rejection, null-tolerance), defaultGuildPath, plus engine validation guards (8 rejections) + teardown safety/happy-path/tolerance/override (4 cases). Subprocess-driven happy path (nsg init / plugin install / codex add) deferred to live smoke testing rather than unit tests — booting the full nsg toolchain per test is too expensive. Live-verify pending fresh daemon restart since the running spider has the pre-edit code loaded; verify manifest staged at .scratch/lab-trial-verify-guild-fixture.yaml ready to post."
  commissionable: false
  justification: sanctum
